name: REACTION ENGINEERING AND PROCESS CONTROL LAB

topics.title: Kinetic Studies in an Isothermal Batch Reactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBL607 - REACTION ENGINEERING AND PROCESS CONTROL LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Kinetic Studies in an Isothermal Batch Reactor # Module Experiment 2: Kinetic Studies in an Isothermal Batch Reactor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) An **isothermal batch reactor** is a closed, well-mixed vessel in which a chemical reaction is carried out under **constant temperature** conditions, with no inflow or outflow of reactants/products during the reaction period. "Kinetic studies" refer to the experimental determination of the **reaction rate law**, i.e., the mathematical relationship between the rate of reaction and the concentration of reactants (and sometimes products), expressed as: $$r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$$ where $r_A$ is the rate of disappearance of reactant A, $k$ is the **rate constant**, $C_A$ is the concentration of A, and $n$ is the **order of reaction**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The isothermal batch reactor is the *simplest and most fundamental* reactor configuration used to extract intrinsic kinetic parameters ($k$, $E$, $n$) because the volume and temperature are held constant, removing these as confounding variables. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are brewing a cup of tea in a closed thermos flask that perfectly maintains its temperature (the *isothermal* condition). You add tea leaves and hot water once, close the lid (the *batch* condition), and then periodically peek inside to measure how strong the tea becomes (the *kinetic study*). Since the thermos keeps the temperature absolutely constant and nothing leaves or enters, the only thing changing is the concentration of tea compounds dissolving into the water. By recording concentration vs. time, you essentially "reverse-engineer" how fast the extraction is happening — this is precisely what we do in a lab batch reactor. > [!NOTE] > **Key Conceptual Point:** A batch reactor is essentially a **differential equation solver** in physical form. The student measures concentration vs. time, and the math tells them the order and rate constant. ### Standard Metrics & Constants - **Reactor volume (V):** Held **constant** at typically **1–2 litres** for lab-scale experiments. - **Operating temperature (T):** Held constant at a chosen setpoint (commonly **303 K, 313 K, or 323 K**). - **Sampling interval ($\Delta t$):** Typically every **2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 45, 60 minutes**. - **Standard analytical methods:** **Titration** (acid-base, iodometric, redox), **conductivity**, **spectrophotometry (absorbance)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **rate constant $k$** is **NOT** truly constant across temperatures — it depends on T via the **Arrhenius equation** $k = A e^{-E/RT}$. However, *within* a single isothermal run, T is fixed, so $k$ is treated as a constant to be determined. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### The Governing Material Balance For a batch reactor, the **general mole balance** for reactant A reduces to: $$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V$$ Because the volume $V$ is **constant** and $N_A = C_A \cdot V$, this simplifies to the cornerstone batch design equation: $$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$$ This is a **single ordinary differential equation (ODE)** with separable variables, the integration of which yields the **integrated rate laws** for different reaction orders. ### Why "Isothermal"? The temperature $T$ directly controls $k$ through the Arrhenius equation. If $T$ drifts during an experiment, $k$ drifts, and the integrated forms no longer apply cleanly. Isothermal operation decouples *chemical kinetics* from *heat transfer*, allowing us to study kinetics alone. ### The Logic Chain for Kinetic Analysis **Step 1 — Data Acquisition:** Measure $C_A$ at discrete times $t_0, t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$. **Step 2 — Choose Analysis Method:** Two classical approaches: - **Integral Method:** Assume an order $n$, integrate the ODE, plot the integrated form vs. $t$. If a **straight line** results, the assumed order is correct. - **Differential Method:** Numerically compute $-dC_A/dt$ from data, then plot $\ln(-dC_A/dt)$ vs. $\ln(C_A)$. Slope = order $n$, intercept = $\ln k$. **Step 3 — Extract Parameters:** From the straight line, obtain slope ($n$) and intercept ($\ln k$). **Step 4 — Validate:** Substitute $k$ and $n$ back into $r_A = k C_A^n$ and check that predicted vs. experimental concentrations agree within $\pm 5\%$. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These parameters ($k$, $n$, $E$) are the *inputs* for designing industrial reactors (CSTR, PFR, packed-bed). Without accurate batch kinetic data, scale-up is essentially guesswork. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Reaction Order (n) | Differential Form | Integrated Form (Linear Plot) | Slope | Units of k | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Zero | $-\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = k$ | $C_{A0} - C_A = k \cdot t$ | $k$ | $\text{mol}\cdot\text{L}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ | | 2 | First | $-\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A$ | $\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = k \cdot t$ | $k$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | | 3 | Second (equal conc.) | $-\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{C_A} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}} = k \cdot t$ | $k$ | $\text{L}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ | | 4 | $n$-th order | $-\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A^n$ | $C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n} = (1-n)k \cdot t$ | varies | $(\text{L}/\text{mol})^{n-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ | | 5 | Arrhenius | $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E}{R}\cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ | $-E/R$ | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Examination Note:** In the KTU 2024 scheme lab exam, students are routinely asked to *write the integrated form*, *identify which plot yields a straight line*, and *compute $k$ from the slope*. Memorise the table above verbatim. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1 — First-Order Integrated Rate Law Starting from the batch reactor design equation for a first-order reaction: $$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A$$ **Step 1:** Separate variables by moving all $C_A$ terms to the left and all $t$ terms to the right: $$\frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k \, dt$$ **Step 2:** Apply the initial condition $C_A = C_{A0}$ at $t = 0$ and integrate from $0$ to $t$: $$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate both integrals: $$\left[ \ln C_A \right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -k \, t$$ **Step 4:** Apply the limits: $$\ln C_A - \ln C_{A0} = -k \, t$$ **Step 5:** Rearrange using the logarithm identity $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$: $$\ln \left( \frac{C_{A0}}{C_A} \right) = k \, t$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \quad C_A = C_{A0} \, e^{-kt}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** $C_A$ decays **exponentially** with time. The **half-life** (time to reach $C_A = C_{A0}/2$) for a first-order reaction is $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k = 0.693/k$, which is **independent** of $C_{A0}$. --- ### Derivation 2 — Second-Order Integrated Rate Law Starting from: $$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A^2$$ **Step 1:** Separate variables: $$\frac{dC_A}{C_A^2} = -k \, dt$$ **Step 2:** Integrate using initial condition $C_A(0) = C_{A0}$: $$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} C_A^{-2} \, dC_A = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the left integral ($\int x^{-2} dx = -x^{-1}$): $$\left[ -\frac{1}{C_A} \right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -k \, t$$ **Step 4:** Apply limits: $$-\frac{1}{C_A} + \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = -k \, t$$ **Step 5:** Multiply by $-1$ and rearrange: $$\frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k \, t$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \quad \frac{1}{C_A} = \frac{1}{C_{A0}} + k \, t}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** A plot of $1/C_A$ vs. $t$ yields a straight line with **slope = $k$** and **intercept = $1/C_{A0}$**. Half-life is $t_{1/2} = 1/(k C_{A0})$, which **depends** on initial concentration — this is the diagnostic that distinguishes 2nd order from 1st order. --- ### Derivation 3 — Zero-Order Integrated Rate Law Starting from: $$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k$$ **Step 1:** Direct integration: $$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} dC_A = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ **Step 2:** Evaluate: $$C_A - C_{A0} = -k \, t$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \quad C_A = C_{A0} - k \, t}$$ > [!NOTE] > A plot of $C_A$ vs. $t$ is a straight line. Concentration drops **linearly** with time. This is rare for homogeneous reactions but common for **heterogeneous catalytic** reactions where the surface is saturated. --- ### Derivation 4 — Half-Life Method (General) The half-life is defined by $C_A = C_{A0}/2$ at $t = t_{1/2}$. Substituting into the general integrated form: $$C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n} = (1-n) k \, t_{1/2}$$ At $C_A = C_{A0}/2$: $$\left( \frac{C_{A0}}{2} \right)^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n} = (1-n) k \, t_{1/2}$$ $$C_{A0}^{1-n} \left[ 2^{n-1} - 1 \right] = (1-n) k \, t_{1/2}$$ $$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \frac{2^{n-1} - 1}{(1-n) k} \cdot C_{A0}^{1-n}}$$ A useful corollary — the **ratio of half-lives at two different initial concentrations** gives the order directly: $$\frac{t_{1/2,1}}{t_{1/2,2}} = \left( \frac{C_{A0,1}}{C_{A0,2}} \right)^{1-n}$$ Solving for $n$: $$\boxed{n = 1 + \frac{\ln(t_{1/2,1}/t_{1/2,2})}{\ln(C_{A0,2}/C_{A0,1})}}$$ --- ### Derivation 5 — Arrhenius Temperature Dependence $$k(T) = A \, e^{-E/RT}$$ Taking the natural log of both sides: $$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$ A plot of $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ yields slope $= -E/R$, intercept $= \ln A$. --- ### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** In an isothermal batch reactor, the saponification of ethyl acetate with NaOH was studied at 35 °C. Initial concentrations: $[NaOH]_0 = [EtAc]_0 = 0.05$ mol/L. Data obtained: | Time (min) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 20 | 40 | 60 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $[NaOH]$ (mol/L) | 0.0500 | 0.0380 | 0.0300 | 0.0200 | 0.0111 | 0.0071 | **Determine the order and rate constant.** **Solution:** Compute $1/C_A$ for each time: | $t$ (min) | $C_A$ | $1/C_A$ (L/mol) | |---|---|---| | 0 | 0.0500 | 20.00 | | 5 | 0.0380 | 26.32 | | 10 | 0.0300 | 33.33 | | 20 | 0.0200 | 50.00 | | 40 | 0.0111 | 90.09 | | 60 | 0.0071 | 140.85 | Plotting $1/C_A$ vs. $t$ gives an excellent straight line. Slope: $$k = \frac{140.85 - 20.00}{60 - 0} = \frac{120.85}{60} = 2.014 \; \text{L/(mol·min)}$$ Convert to SI: $k = 2.014 / 60 = 0.0336 \; \text{L/(mol·s)}$. $$\boxed{n = 2, \quad k = 2.014 \; \text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·min}^{-1}}$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Block-Level Functional Architecture — Experimental Setup ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1[Reactor Assembly] R[Glass Batch Reactor
Capacity 1 to 2 Litres
Stirred Tank Type] TC[Temperature Controller
PID Type with J Type Thermocouple] HM[Heating Mantle
Electric Resistance Heating] ST[Magnetic / Overhead Stirrer
Constant RPM Setpoint] CS[Cooling Coil
For Heat Removal] end subgraph S2[Instrumentation and Sampling] TH[Thermometer / Sensor
Reads Reactor T in Degree C] SP[Sampling Port
Syringe Withdraws 5 mL Aliquots] QC[Quench Tank
Stops Reaction Instantly] end subgraph S3[Analytical Section] TI[Titration Setup
Burette with Standard HCl or NaOH] SP2[Spectrophotometer
Optional UV VIS Method] CD[Conductivity Meter
Optional Online Method] DA[Data Acquisition Sheet
Time Versus Concentration] end subgraph S4[Analysis Module] IM[Integral Method
Test Zero First Second Order] DM[Differential Method
Plot Ln Rate Versus Ln C] AR[Arrhenius Plot
Multiple Temperature Runs] end HM --> R TC --> HM TC --> TH ST --> R CS --> R R --> SP SP --> QC QC --> TI QC --> SP2 QC --> CD TI --> DA SP2 --> DA CD --> DA DA --> IM DA --> DM DA --> AR ``` ### Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Determining Reaction Order ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Obtain C_A Versus t Data] --> B{Compute Three Quantities} B --> B1[C_A Versus t] B --> B2[ln C_A Versus t] B --> B3[1 divided by C_A Versus t] B1 --> C1{Straight Line?} B2 --> C2{Straight Line?} B3 --> C3{Straight Line?} C1 -->|Yes| Z[Zero Order Confirmed, slope equals minus k] C2 -->|Yes| F[First Order Confirmed, slope equals minus k] C3 -->|Yes| S[Second Order Confirmed, slope equals plus k] C1 -->|No| D[None of These, Try nth Order or Differential Method] C2 -->|No| D C3 -->|No| D ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Why is an isothermal batch reactor preferred for kinetic studies?** **Model Answer:** An isothermal batch reactor is preferred because: 1. The temperature is held **constant**, eliminating its effect on the rate constant $k$ and simplifying the rate equation. 2. The volume is also **constant**, so the mole balance reduces directly to a first-order ODE in $C_A$ only. 3. It allows accurate measurement of concentration vs. time data, from which intrinsic kinetic parameters ($k$, $n$) can be extracted without interference from flow or heat-transfer effects. 4. It is the **simplest** reactor to operate and mathematically analyse. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] For a first-order reaction $A \to B$ in an isothermal batch reactor, write the integrated rate law and the expression for half-life.** **Model Answer:** Integrated rate law: $$\ln\left(\frac{C_{A0}}{C_A}\right) = k \, t \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad C_A = C_{A0} \, e^{-kt}$$ Half-life (set $C_A = C_{A0}/2$): $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$ The half-life is **independent** of initial concentration — this is a key diagnostic property of first-order reactions. --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the integrated rate law for a second-order reaction $2A \to \text{Products}$ (with stoichiometry 1:1, equal initial concentrations) carried out in an isothermal batch reactor. Show that the half-life depends on the initial concentration. **(b) [7 Marks]** In an isothermal batch reactor at 320 K, the following data were obtained for a liquid-phase reaction: | $t$ (s) | 0 | 100 | 200 | 400 | 800 | 1200 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $C_A$ (mol/L) | 1.00 | 0.80 | 0.67 | 0.50 | 0.33 | 0.25 | Determine the **reaction order** and the **rate constant $k$** at 320 K. --- **Solution to (a):** For $2A \to \text{Products}$, the rate is: $$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k \, C_A^2$$ Separate variables: $$\frac{dC_A}{C_A^2} = -k \, dt$$ Integrate from $C_{A0}$ at $t=0$ to $C_A$ at $t$: $$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A}{C_A^2} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ $$\left[-\frac{1}{C_A}\right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -k \, t$$ $$-\frac{1}{C_A} + \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = -k \, t$$ $$\boxed{\frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k \, t}$$ For half-life, substitute $C_A = C_{A0}/2$: $$\frac{2}{C_{A0}} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k \, t_{1/2}$$ $$\frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k \, t_{1/2}$$ $$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{k \, C_{A0}}}$$ This clearly shows that $t_{1/2}$ **depends on** $C_{A0}$ — doubling $C_{A0}$ halves the half-life. This is in stark contrast to first-order reactions where $t_{1/2}$ is independent of $C_{A0}$. --- **Solution to (b):** Test all three integrated forms by computing the relevant quantities. | $t$ (s) | $C_A$ | $\ln C_A$ | $1/C_A$ | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 1.00 | 0.000 | 1.00 | | 100 | 0.80 | $-0.223$ | 1.25 | | 200 | 0.67 | $-0.400$ | 1.49 | | 400 | 0.50 | $-0.693$ | 2.00 | | 800 | 0.33 | $-1.109$ | 3.03 | | 1200 | 0.25 | $-1.386$ | 4.00 | Checking linearity: - $C_A$ vs. $t$: not linear (curves downward). - $\ln C_A$ vs. $t$: differences are $-0.223, -0.177, -0.293, -0.416, -0.277$ — not constant slope. - $1/C_A$ vs. $t$: differences are $0.25, 0.24, 0.51, 1.03, 0.97$ — average slope around $0.0025$ L/(mol·s). More precise calculation of slope for $1/C_A$ vs. $t$ using least-squares (or end-points): $$k = \frac{4.00 - 1.00}{1200 - 0} = \frac{3.00}{1200} = 0.0025 \; \text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ Wait — let us check first-order by computing rate of decay. From $t=0$ to $t=1200$: $$\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = \ln(1.00/0.25) = \ln 4 = 1.386$$ If first-order, $k = 1.386 / 1200 = 0.001155 \; \text{s}^{-1}$. Checking: at $t=400$, predicted $C_A = 1.00 \cdot e^{-0.001155 \cdot 400} = e^{-0.462} = 0.630$, but observed = 0.50. ❌ Checking second-order: at $t=400$, predicted $1/C_A = 1.00 + 0.0025 \cdot 400 = 2.00 \Rightarrow C_A = 0.50$. ✓ Matches exactly! $$\boxed{n = 2, \quad k = 0.0025 \; \text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1} \text{ at } 320 \text{ K}}$$ --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **differential method** of analysing batch reactor data. How is the order of reaction determined from the plot? **(b) [7 Marks]** The following data were obtained for the decomposition of $A$ in an isothermal batch reactor: | $t$ (min) | 0 | 10 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 90 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $C_A$ (mol/L) | 2.00 | 1.46 | 1.10 | 0.65 | 0.40 | 0.20 | Using the **half-life method**, determine the order of the reaction. --- **Solution to (a):** The **differential method** does not assume an order in advance. From raw $C_A$ vs. $t$ data, the instantaneous rate $-dC_A/dt$ is computed numerically (e.g., using finite differences, graphical differentiation, or polynomial fitting). Starting from the general power-law rate form: $$-r_A = -\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k \, C_A^n$$ Take the natural logarithm of both sides: $$\ln\left(-\frac{dC_A}{dt}\right) = \ln k + n \ln C_A$$ This is linear in the form $Y = \text{intercept} + \text{slope} \cdot X$, where: - $Y = \ln(-dC_A/dt)$ - $X = \ln C_A$ - **Slope $= n$** (order of reaction) - **Intercept $= \ln k$** Procedure: 1. Tabulate $(C_A, t)$ data. 2. Compute $-dC_A/dt$ at each interior point using a numerical formula such as the three-point central difference: $\left(\frac{dC_A}{dt}\right)_i \approx \frac{C_{A,i+1} - C_{A,i-1}}{t_{i+1} - t_{i-1}}$. 3. Compute $\ln C_A$ and $\ln(-dC_A/dt)$. 4. Plot $\ln(-dC_A/dt)$ vs. $\ln C_A$; the slope is $n$. **Advantage:** It does not require any *a priori* assumption of order. **Disadvantage:** Numerical differentiation amplifies experimental scatter. --- **Solution to (b):** We use two starting concentrations. Take $C_{A0,1} = 2.00$ mol/L. Find time to reach $C_A = 1.00$ mol/L (half of 2.00): By interpolation between $t=10$ ($C_A=1.46$) and $t=20$ ($C_A=1.10$): $$t_{1/2,1} = 10 + \frac{1.46 - 1.00}{1.46 - 1.10} \cdot 10 = 10 + \frac{0.46}{0.36} \cdot 10 = 10 + 12.78 = 22.78 \; \text{min}$$ For a second trial, take $C_{A0,2} = 1.10$ mol/L (at $t=20$ min). Find time to reach $C_A = 0.55$ mol/L (half of 1.10): Between $t=20$ ($C_A=1.10$) and $t=40$ ($C_A=0.65$): $$t_{1/2,2} = 20 + \frac{1.10 - 0.55}{1.10 - 0.65} \cdot 20 = 20 + \frac{0.55}{0.45} \cdot 20 = 20 + 24.44 = 44.44 \; \text{min}$$ Now apply: $$n = 1 + \frac{\ln(t_{1/2,1}/t_{1/2,2})}{\ln(C_{A0,2}/C_{A0,1})}$$ $$n = 1 + \frac{\ln(22.78/44.44)}{\ln(1.10/2.00)} = 1 + \frac{\ln(0.5126)}{\ln(0.5500)}$$ $$n = 1 + \frac{-0.6685}{-0.5978} = 1 + 1.118 = 2.118$$ $$\boxed{n \approx 2 \; (\text{second-order reaction})}$$ Cross-check: ratio of half-lives $= 22.78/44.44 \approx 0.513$. For second order, halving initial concentration should **double** the half-life — here $C_{A0}$ went from $2.00$ to $1.10$ (ratio $0.55$), and $t_{1/2}$ went from $22.78$ to $44.44$ (ratio $\approx 1.95$, close to expected $1/0.55 \approx 1.82$). Confirmed second-order. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $-dC_A/dt$. Since $C_A$ decreases, the derivative is negative; the rate of disappearance is the *magnitude*. Write $\ln(-dC_A/dt)$, not $\ln(dC_A/dt)$. > 2. **Plotting $C_A$ vs. $t$ but expecting a straight line for second order** — for 2nd order, plot $1/C_A$ vs. $t$. Wrong axis choice → wrong slope → wrong answer → **zero marks** for the analysis. > 3. **Mixing up the slope sign** in $\ln C_A$ vs. $t$ for first order — the slope is **$-k$**, not $+k$. Always write $\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = kt$ explicitly to avoid sign errors. > 4. **Unit errors for $k$:** Zero order: mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹; First order: s⁻¹; Second order: L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹. Examiners *will* deduct marks if units are wrong or missing. > 5. **Forgetting to convert time units** (min vs. s) when computing $k$. A common $60\times$ factor error. > 6. **Not stating the assumption that volume is constant** — this is implicit in deriving $-dC_A/dt = r_A$ but must be mentioned. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Batch reactor design equation** (constant $V$): $-dC_A/dt = r_A = k C_A^n$. This is the single most important equation. - **Integral method** = assume $n$, plot integrated form, see if straight line. - **Differential method** = no assumption, plot $\ln(-dC_A/dt)$ vs. $\ln C_A$; slope = $n$. - **Half-life method** = measure $t_{1/2}$ at two different $C_{A0}$, use $n = 1 + \ln(t_{1/2,1}/t_{1/2,2})/\ln(C_{A0,2}/C_{A0,1})$. - **First-order plot:** $\ln C_A$ vs. $t$ → straight line, slope = $-k$. - **Second-order plot:** $1/C_A$ vs. $t$ → straight line, slope = $+k$. - **Zero-order plot:** $C_A$ vs. $t$ → straight line, slope = $-k$. - **Half-life signatures:** 1st order → independent of $C_{A0}$; 2nd order → $\propto 1/C_{A0}$; zero order → $\propto C_{A0}$. - **Arrhenius plot:** $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ → straight line, slope = $-E/R$. - **Typical lab reactions:** Saponification of ethyl acetate (2nd order), hydrolysis of sucrose (pseudo-1st order with acid catalyst), decomposition of H₂O₂ (1st order), iodination of acetone (acid-catalysed, pseudo-1st order). - **Isothermal condition is critical** — even $\pm 1\,°\text{C}$ drift can give $\pm 5\%$ error in $k$. - **Quenching the sample** (e.g., by cooling or adding inhibitor) instantly stops reaction at the sampling instant, "freezing" $C_A$ for analysis. - **Always plot three candidate integrated forms** (zero, first, second) before concluding the order — never assume. - **Units of $k$** depend on $n$: $\text{time}^{-1}$ for $n=0$? No — $\text{conc} \cdot \text{time}^{-1}$ for $n=0$. Correct unit formula: $\text{(L/mol)}^{n-1} \cdot \text{time}^{-1}$.

topics.title: Kinetic Studies in an Isothermal Semi-Batch Reactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL607 - REACTION ENGINEERING AND PROCESS CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Kinetic Studies in an Isothermal Semi-Batch Reactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semi-Batch Reactor (SBR):** A semi-batch reactor is a hybrid reactor configuration in which **one reactant is initially charged into the vessel** while the **second reactant is fed continuously** over a period of time. The reaction mixture volume therefore changes with time, but unlike a CSTR, there is no outflow stream. The reaction is carried out under **isothermal conditions** ($T = \text{constant}$), which simplifies the kinetic analysis by removing the temperature variable from the rate expression.

A semi-batch reactor is also termed a **fed-batch reactor** and is widely used when:
- One reactant is highly reactive/exothermic and must be added slowly to control selectivity or heat.
- It is desired to maintain a low concentration of a key reactant to suppress side reactions.
- The reaction stoichiometry favors a product when one reactant is kept in excess gradually.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap and Bucket" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine filling a bucket using a tap, but the bucket has a small hole at the bottom through which water continuously leaks out. The water level in the bucket depends on how fast the tap pours and how fast water leaks. In a semi-batch reactor, the **tap = feed stream of reactant B** (flowing in), and the **leak = chemical consumption of A and B by reaction** (no physical outflow, only depletion). The bucket itself is the reactor volume, which grows initially and then stabilizes once the feed stops or reaction consumes the incoming species.

This analogy makes it clear that:
- The **volume increases monotonically** during feeding (in pure liquid systems).
- The **concentration of the fed species B** stays low (good for selectivity).
- The **concentration of the initially charged species A** decreases due to both reaction **and** dilution.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical SI Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** | Used in Arrhenius equation |
| Standard temperature | $T_0$ | **298.15 K (25 °C)** | Reference isothermal condition |
| Density of water | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg·m⁻³** | Used when feed is dilute aqueous |
| Molar mass of water | $M_w$ | **18.015 g·mol⁻¹** | For dilution calculations |

### 1.4 Why "Isothermal"?

> [!NOTE]
> In a KTU lab experiment, the reactor is **jacketed and connected to a constant-temperature bath** so that heat generated (or absorbed) by the reaction is removed (or supplied) almost instantly. As a result, $T$ inside the reactor remains essentially constant, and the **rate constant $k$ does not vary with time**. This is what makes it possible to determine the **true concentration-dependent kinetics** of the reaction without overlapping with temperature effects.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

A semi-batch reactor operating isothermally for the reaction $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$ follows this conceptual logic:

1. **Initial Charge:** Species A (or B) is loaded into the reactor at $t = 0$ with initial volume $V_0$ and concentration $C_{A,0}$.
2. **Feed Initiation:** Species B (or A) is introduced through a calibrated pump or burette at a known volumetric flow rate $F$ (L·s⁻¹) and inlet concentration $C_{B,\text{in}}$.
3. **Reaction Onset:** As soon as B enters, it reacts with A. Heat released is absorbed by the jacket — $T$ remains constant.
4. **Volume Evolution:** Total volume $V(t) = V_0 + F t$ (assuming constant density feed).
5. **Sampling:** Small aliquots are withdrawn at regular intervals and analyzed (titration, conductivity, absorbance) to determine residual concentration of A or B.
6. **Data Treatment:** Concentration vs. time data is fitted to integrated rate laws to obtain **reaction order $n$** and **rate constant $k$**.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why feed gradually?** A slow feed keeps the concentration of the limiting/key reactant low, giving better control of selectivity in complex reactions (e.g., nitration, polymerization, enzymatic processes).
- **Why isothermal?** Removes $T$ from the rate equation, isolating the effect of $C_A$ and $C_B$ on rate.
- **How to analyze data?** The volume change with time must be explicitly accounted for in the mole balance — a feature unique to semi-batch reactors.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the master reference for all semi-batch kinetic calculations. Memorize the variable definitions and the units.**

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V(t) = V_0 + F \cdot t$ | Volume-time relation (constant-density feed) |
| 2 | $N_A(t) = C_A(t) \cdot V(t)$ | Total moles of A in reactor at time $t$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = -r_A \cdot V(t)$ | Mole balance on A (no outflow, no A in feed) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = -\dfrac{r_A}{V}\dfrac{dV}{dt}\text{ terms}$ | Expanding with product rule |
| 5 | $r_A = k \, C_A^{\alpha} \, C_B^{\beta}$ | General rate law (orders $\alpha$, $\beta$) |
| 6 | $C_B(t) = \dfrac{F \, C_{B,\text{in}} \, t - \xi(t)}{V(t)}$ | Concentration of fed species after reaction extent $\xi$ |
| 7 | $k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius equation (used when temperature is varied) |
| 8 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{2^{\,n-1}-1}{(n-1) \, k \, C_{A,0}^{\,n-1}}$ | Half-life for $n$-th order reaction ($n \neq 1$) |
| 9 | $\ln t_{1/2} = \ln\!\left[\dfrac{2^{\,n-1}-1}{(n-1)\,k}\right] - (n-1)\ln C_{A,0}$ | Linearized form for order determination |
| 10 | $\text{Slope of } \ln t_{1/2} \text{ vs } \ln C_{A,0} = -(n-1)$ | Method of half-lives |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table convention:** All vertical bars in math expressions inside the table have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve the markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Semi-batch reactors are industrial workhorses in:
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Controlled addition of reagents in multi-step synthesis.
- **Polymerization:** Emulsion polymerization where monomer is fed over hours.
- **Biotechnology:** Fed-batch fermentation for recombinant proteins, antibiotics, and vaccines.
- **Fine chemicals:** Nitration of aromatics, sulfonation, halogenation.

In all these applications, the **kinetic parameters** ($k$, $n$, $E_a$) obtained from a small-scale lab semi-batch experiment are used to design and scale up industrial reactors.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 General Mole Balance for a Semi-Batch Reactor

Starting from the **general mole balance** applied to species A in a control volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accumulation} &= \text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Consumption by Reaction} \\
\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} &= F_{\text{in}} \, C_{A,\text{in}} - F_{\text{out}} \, C_A - r_A \, V
\end{aligned}
$$

For a semi-batch reactor:
- $F_{\text{out}} = 0$ (no outflow stream)
- $C_{A,\text{in}} = 0$ (A is not in the feed; B is being fed)

So the balance reduces to:

$$
\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = - r_A \, V(t)
$$

### 3.2 Expanding the Accumulation Term

Using the product rule with $N_A = C_A \cdot V(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} &= \dfrac{d}{dt}\bigl(C_A V\bigr) \\
&= V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A \dfrac{dV}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating both expressions for $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt}$:

$$
V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A \dfrac{dV}{dt} = - r_A \, V
$$

Dividing throughout by $V$:

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + \dfrac{C_A}{V}\dfrac{dV}{dt} = - r_A
$$

Substituting $V = V_0 + Ft$ so that $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = F$:

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + \dfrac{F \, C_A}{V_0 + F t} = - r_A
$$

This is the **fundamental design equation** for an isothermal semi-batch reactor.

### 3.3 Special Case: First-Order Irreversible Reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$

For $r_A = k C_A$, and assuming no volume change (slow feed → $V \approx V_0$, valid for small additions):

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + \dfrac{F C_A}{V_0} = - k C_A
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = -\!\left(k + \dfrac{F}{V_0}\right) C_A
$$

This is a **linear first-order ODE** with constant coefficients, integrated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{C_{A,0}}^{C_A} \dfrac{dC_A}{C_A} &= -\int_0^t \!\left(k + \dfrac{F}{V_0}\right) dt \\
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_A}{C_{A,0}}\right) &= -\!\left(k + \dfrac{F}{V_0}\right) t \\
C_A(t) &= C_{A,0} \, \exp\!\left[-\!\left(k + \dfrac{F}{V_0}\right) t\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The effective first-order rate constant in a semi-batch reactor is **enhanced** by the dilution term $\dfrac{F}{V_0}$. Students commonly forget this term and consequently report a $k$ value that is too large.

### 3.4 Special Case: Second-Order Reaction $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$

For $r_A = k C_A C_B$ with B fed into A:

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + \dfrac{F C_A}{V_0 + F t} = - k C_A C_B
$$

This ODE generally requires **numerical integration** (e.g., Runge-Kutta 4th order) because the right-hand side is non-linear. However, if B is added in **large stoichiometric excess** so $C_B \approx \text{const} = C_{B,\text{in}}$ (pseudo-order approximation), it reduces to:

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = - k' C_A \quad \text{where} \quad k' = k C_{B,\text{in}}
$$

and the integrated form is identical to the first-order case.

### 3.5 Half-Life Method for Order Determination

For a reaction of order $n \neq 1$, the half-life depends on initial concentration:

$$
t_{1/2} = \dfrac{2^{\,n-1} - 1}{(n-1) \, k \, C_{A,0}^{\,n-1}}
$$

Take the natural logarithm:

$$
\ln t_{1/2} = \ln\!\left[\dfrac{2^{\,n-1} - 1}{(n-1) k}\right] - (n-1) \ln C_{A,0}
$$

A plot of $\ln t_{1/2}$ (y-axis) vs. $\ln C_{A,0}$ (x-axis) yields:
- **Slope** = $-(n-1)$ → so $n = 1 - \text{slope}$
- **Intercept** = $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2^{\,n-1} - 1}{(n-1) k}\right]$ → from which $k$ is calculated.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** The saponification of ethyl acetate by NaOH is studied in an isothermal semi-batch reactor at 25 °C. Initial charge: $V_0 = 0.500$ L of NaOH at $C_{A,0} = 0.100$ mol·L⁻¹. Ethyl acetate (B) is fed at $F = 0.005$ L·min⁻¹ with $C_{B,\text{in}} = 0.500$ mol·L⁻¹. The reaction is second-order overall, $r_A = k C_A C_B$, with $k = 0.110$ L·mol⁻¹·min⁻¹. Find $C_A$ at $t = 30$ min.

**Step 1 — Identify governing equation:**

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + \dfrac{F C_A}{V_0 + F t} = - k C_A C_B
$$

**Step 2 — Approximate $C_B$ using stoichiometry:**

Total moles of B added in 30 min:

$$
N_{B,\text{added}} = F \cdot C_{B,\text{in}} \cdot t = 0.005 \times 0.500 \times 30 = 0.075 \text{ mol}
$$

If reaction has consumed $x$ mol of A and B, then $C_B = (0.075 - x)/(V_0 + Ft)$.

**Step 3 — Iterate (forward Euler, $\Delta t = 5$ min):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = 0 &: \quad C_A = 0.100,\; C_B = 0,\; V = 0.500 \text{ L},\; r_A = 0 \\
t = 5 &: \quad V = 0.525 \text{ L},\; N_B = 0.0125 \text{ mol},\; x \approx 0 \\
&\quad C_B \approx 0.0238,\; r_A = 0.110 \times 0.100 \times 0.0238 = 2.62 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\quad \Delta C_A = -2.62 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 = -1.31 \times 10^{-3} \\
&\quad C_A = 0.0987 \text{ mol·L}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Continuing the iteration to $t = 30$ min yields $C_A \approx 0.083 \text{ mol·L}^{-1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> In a KTU lab, students typically plot $C_A$ vs. $t$ and fit the curve using **polyfit or nonlinear regression** (graph paper / spreadsheet) — the numerical example above is for conceptual clarity.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define an isothermal semi-batch reactor. How does it differ from a batch reactor and a CSTR?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An isothermal semi-batch reactor is a reactor in which one reactant is initially charged and the second is fed continuously over time, with the temperature maintained constant by a thermostatic jacket. It differs from:
- **Batch reactor:** No feed stream; volume is constant; all reactants charged at $t = 0$.
- **CSTR:** Has continuous outflow; volume is constant; operates at steady state.

The semi-batch reactor is a **hybrid** with continuous feed but no outflow, leading to a time-varying volume.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference with batch, 1 mark for difference with CSTR.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the design equation (mole balance) for species A in a semi-batch reactor where A is the initial charge and B is fed.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For A initially charged (no A in feed, no outflow):

$$
\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = - r_A \, V(t)
$$

Expanding the left side using the product rule and noting $V(t) = V_0 + Ft$:

$$
V(t) \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A \dfrac{dV}{dt} = - r_A \, V(t)
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + \dfrac{F \, C_A}{V_0 + F t} = - r_A
$$

This is the fundamental semi-batch design equation.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for mole balance, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final form.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** *The saponification of ethyl acetate with NaOH is carried out in an isothermal semi-batch reactor at 35 °C. The NaOH (species A) is initially charged with $V_0 = 1.0$ L, $C_{A,0} = 0.20$ mol·L⁻¹. Ethyl acetate (B) is fed at $F = 0.010$ L·min⁻¹ with $C_{B,\text{in}} = 1.0$ mol·L⁻¹. Assume the reaction is second-order overall: $r_A = k C_A C_B$ with $k = 0.080$ L·mol⁻¹·min⁻¹. The density of the mixture is constant.*

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the semi-batch design equation for $C_A(t)$ and explain why the term $\dfrac{F C_A}{V_0 + F t}$ appears in it.

**Model Solution:**

Apply the general mole balance on A (no A in feed, no outflow):

$$
\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = - r_A \, V(t) \quad \cdots (1)
$$

Expand the accumulation term:

$$
\dfrac{d(C_A V)}{dt} = V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A \dfrac{dV}{dt} \quad \cdots (2)
$$

Equate (1) and (2):

$$
V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A \dfrac{dV}{dt} = - r_A \, V
$$

Divide by $V$ and substitute $V = V_0 + Ft$:

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + \dfrac{F \, C_A}{V_0 + F t} = - r_A
$$

Substituting $r_A = k C_A C_B$:

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + \dfrac{F C_A}{V_0 + F t} = - k C_A C_B \quad \cdots (*)
$$

**Why the $\dfrac{F C_A}{V_0 + F t}$ term appears:** This term arises from the **rate at which species A is being diluted** by the incoming feed of B. Even without any chemical reaction, the concentration of A would fall as the reactor volume grows from $V_0$ to $V_0 + Ft$. The dilution rate is $\dfrac{1}{V}\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{F}{V_0 + Ft}$, multiplied by $C_A$ to give the dilution contribution.

*(Marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for explaining the dilution term, 2 for final substituted equation.)*

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Using the pseudo-first-order approximation (B in large excess, $C_B \approx C_{B,\text{in}}$), obtain $C_A(t)$ analytically. Calculate the time required for $C_A$ to drop to 0.10 mol·L⁻¹.

**Model Solution:**

With $C_B \approx C_{B,\text{in}} = 1.0$ mol·L⁻¹, the rate becomes pseudo-first-order:

$$
r_A = k' C_A, \quad \text{where} \quad k' = k \cdot C_{B,\text{in}} = 0.080 \times 1.0 = 0.080 \text{ min}^{-1}
$$

The design equation $(*)$ becomes:

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + \dfrac{F C_A}{V_0 + F t} = - k' C_A
$$

Rearrange:

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = -\!\left(k' + \dfrac{F}{V_0 + F t}\right) C_A
$$

This is a **separable ODE**:

$$
\int_{C_{A,0}}^{C_A} \dfrac{dC_A}{C_A} = -\int_0^t \!\left(k' + \dfrac{F}{V_0 + F t}\right) dt
$$

The right-hand integral splits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_A}{C_{A,0}}\right) &= -k' t - \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_0 + Ft}{V_0}\right) \\
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_A}{C_{A,0}}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_0 + Ft}{V_0}\right) &= -k' t \\
\ln\!\left[\dfrac{C_A (V_0 + Ft)}{C_{A,0} V_0}\right] &= -k' t
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $C_A$:

$$
C_A(t) = \dfrac{C_{A,0} V_0}{V_0 + F t} \, e^{-k' t}
$$

**Numerical calculation for $C_A = 0.10$ mol·L⁻¹:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.10 &= \dfrac{0.20 \times 1.0}{1.0 + 0.010 \, t} \, e^{-0.080 \, t} \\
\dfrac{0.10 (1.0 + 0.010 t)}{0.20} &= e^{-0.080 t} \\
0.5 (1.0 + 0.010 t) &= e^{-0.080 t} \\
0.5 + 0.005 t &= e^{-0.080 t}
\end{aligned}
$$

This transcendental equation is solved iteratively. Try $t = 5$ min:

$$
\text{LHS} = 0.5 + 0.025 = 0.525, \quad \text{RHS} = e^{-0.40} = 0.670 \;\; (\text{RHS} > \text{LHS})
$$

Try $t = 7$ min:

$$
\text{LHS} = 0.5 + 0.035 = 0.535, \quad \text{RHS} = e^{-0.56} = 0.571 \;\; (\text{RHS} > \text{LHS})
$$

Try $t = 8$ min:

$$
\text{LHS} = 0.5 + 0.040 = 0.540, \quad \text{RHS} = e^{-0.64} = 0.527 \;\; (\text{RHS} < \text{LHS})
$$

By linear interpolation between 7 and 8 min:

$$
t \approx 7 + \dfrac{0.571 - 0.535}{0.571 - 0.527} \times 1 = 7 + \dfrac{0.036}{0.044} \approx 7.8 \text{ min}
$$

**Answer:** $t \approx 7.8$ min.

*(Marks: 3 for analytical integration, 2 for deriving the closed-form $C_A(t)$, 2 for the numerical calculation.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** *A kinetic study of the reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ is conducted in an isothermal semi-batch reactor. The initial charge is $V_0 = 0.750$ L, $C_{A,0} = 0.150$ mol·L⁻¹. The feed (containing only solvent, no A or B) is introduced at $F = 0.004$ L·min⁻¹ to maintain constant dilution. The temperature is 30 °C.*

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the expression for $C_A(t)$ assuming first-order kinetics, $r_A = k C_A$, with the feed being pure diluent.

**Model Solution:**

Mole balance on A (no A in feed, no outflow):

$$
\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = - r_A V
$$

Expanding $N_A = C_A V$:

$$
V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A \dfrac{dV}{dt} = - k C_A V
$$

Since the feed is pure diluent, $V(t) = V_0 + F t$, and $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = F$:

$$
(V_0 + F t) \dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + F C_A = - k C_A (V_0 + F t)
$$

$$
\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = - k C_A - \dfrac{F C_A}{V_0 + F t} = -\!\left(k + \dfrac{F}{V_0 + F t}\right) C_A
$$

Separate variables:

$$
\int_{C_{A,0}}^{C_A} \dfrac{dC_A}{C_A} = -\int_0^t \!\left(k + \dfrac{F}{V_0 + F t}\right) dt
$$

Integrate:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_A}{C_{A,0}}\right) = - k t - \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_0 + F t}{V_0}\right)
$$

Exponentiate:

$$
\dfrac{C_A}{C_{A,0}} = \dfrac{V_0}{V_0 + F t} \, e^{-k t}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_A(t) = \dfrac{C_{A,0} V_0}{V_0 + F t} \, e^{-k t}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The factor $\dfrac{V_0}{V_0 + F t}$ accounts for the dilution of A by the incoming feed stream (pure solvent), while the exponential $e^{-kt}$ accounts for the chemical reaction.

*(Marks: 4 for derivation, 2 for integration, 1 for final boxed answer.)*

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Using the **method of half-lives**, derive the working equation to determine the reaction order $n$. If two experiments give $C_{A,0}^{(1)} = 0.10$ mol·L⁻¹ with $t_{1/2}^{(1)} = 18.0$ min and $C_{A,0}^{(2)} = 0.05$ mol·L⁻¹ with $t_{1/2}^{(2)} = 35.5$ min, find $n$ and $k$.

**Model Solution:**

For an $n$-th order reaction ($n \neq 1$), the half-life is:

$$
t_{1/2} = \dfrac{2^{\,n-1} - 1}{(n-1) \, k \, C_{A,0}^{\,n-1}}
$$

Take natural log:

$$
\ln t_{1/2} = \ln\!\left[\dfrac{2^{\,n-1} - 1}{(n-1) k}\right] - (n-1) \ln C_{A,0}
$$

This is a straight line: $y = mx + c$ with slope $m = -(n-1)$ and $x = \ln C_{A,0}$.

**Compute the slope from the two data points:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln C_{A,0}^{(1)} &= \ln(0.10) = -2.303 \\
\ln C_{A,0}^{(2)} &= \ln(0.05) = -2.996 \\
\ln t_{1/2}^{(1)} &= \ln(18.0) = 2.890 \\
\ln t_{1/2}^{(2)} &= \ln(35.5) = 3.570
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
m = \dfrac{\ln t_{1/2}^{(2)} - \ln t_{1/2}^{(1)}}{\ln C_{A,0}^{(2)} - \ln C_{A,0}^{(1)}} = \dfrac{3.570 - 2.890}{-2.996 - (-2.303)} = \dfrac{0.680}{-0.693} = -0.981
$$

**Reaction order:**

$$
n - 1 = 0.981 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n \approx 1.98 \approx 2
$$

So the reaction is **second order**.

**Rate constant $k$** (using experiment 1):

$$
t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{k C_{A,0}} \quad \text{(for } n = 2 \text{)}
$$

$$
k = \dfrac{1}{t_{1/2} \cdot C_{A,0}} = \dfrac{1}{18.0 \times 0.10} = 0.556 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·min}^{-1}
$$

**Verification with experiment 2:**

$$
k = \dfrac{1}{35.5 \times 0.05} = 0.563 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·min}^{-1}
$$

Average $k \approx 0.56$ L·mol⁻¹·min⁻¹.

*(Marks: 3 for deriving half-life equation, 2 for slope calculation, 2 for $n$ and $k$.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dilution term** $\dfrac{F C_A}{V_0 + F t}$ in the semi-batch design equation. The single most common error — students often write only the batch reactor equation and lose 3–4 marks instantly.
> 2. **Sign convention errors:** Consumption of A is *negative*; feed addition is *positive*. The design equation must have consistent signs.
> 3. **Unit confusion:** $k$ for a first-order reaction has units of min⁻¹, while for a second-order reaction it has units of L·mol⁻¹·min⁻¹. Wrong units = full marks deducted in numerical answers.
> 4. **Linearization errors:** Plotting $\ln C_A$ vs. $t$ alone does NOT confirm first-order kinetics in a semi-batch — the *correct* linearization includes the dilution term. Always plot the full linearized form.
> 5. **Neglecting temperature control:** Stating $T = 30\;°C$ without mentioning the thermostatic jacket shows incomplete understanding and may cost 1 mark in definition-type questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Semi-batch reactor = batch (no outflow) + feed (continuous inflow).** Volume changes linearly with time: $V(t) = V_0 + Ft$.
- **Design equation:** $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} + \dfrac{F C_A}{V_0 + F t} = - r_A$. The dilution term is the **signature** of a semi-batch reactor.
- **Isothermal assumption** means $k$ is **constant** throughout the experiment — only concentration effects are studied.
- **Three main methods** to determine kinetics from $C_A$ vs. $t$ data: **Integral method**, **Differential method**, **Half-life method**.
- **Half-life method** is the most robust for non-integer orders because it requires no assumption of $n$ upfront.
- **Pseudo-first-order** approximation simplifies analysis when one reactant is in large excess.
- **Typical lab reaction:** Saponification of ethyl acetate by NaOH — second-order overall, isothermal, well-characterized.
- **Always** record $V_0$, $F$, $C_{A,0}$, $C_{B,\text{in}}$, and $T$ before starting the experiment.
- **Always** plot both raw $C_A$ vs. $t$ and the appropriate linearized form (e.g., $\ln C_A$ vs. $t$ for first-order after dilution correction).
- **Report** $k$ with correct units, $n$ as a dimensionless number, and $E_a$ in kJ·mol⁻¹ if temperature variation is included.
- **Industrial relevance:** Polymerization, fermentation, nitration, pharmaceutical synthesis — all rely on semi-batch kinetic data for safe scale-up.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinetic Studies in a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL607 - REACTION ENGINEERING AND PROCESS CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Kinetic Studies in a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)**, also referred to as a **Mixed-Flow Reactor (MFR)** or **Backmix Reactor**, is an idealised reactor configuration in which the contents are perfectly and instantaneously mixed such that the composition, temperature, and concentration at any point inside the reactor are identical to those at the outlet stream.

For **Kinetic Studies**, the CSTR operates under **steady-state** and **isothermal** conditions, and is used as an experimental tool to determine the **reaction rate (-r_A)**, the **rate constant (k)**, and the **reaction order (n)** of a homogeneous chemical reaction. The fundamental mass balance that governs the reactor at steady state is:

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A \cdot V = 0$$

where the rate of disappearance is conventionally written as a positive quantity: $-r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The CSTR is the *preferred* laboratory reactor for intrinsic kinetics determination because the **measured outlet concentration equals the concentration everywhere inside the vessel**, allowing the rate to be computed directly from a *single steady-state measurement* without spatial integration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **large washing tub** filled with soapy water (the reacting mixture). You keep pouring in dirty water from the top at a constant rate (the **feed**) while simultaneously draining out the mixed water from the bottom (the **product**). Because the agitator (the **impeller**) is spinning vigorously, the moment a drop of new dirty water enters, it is instantly dispersed throughout the entire tub.

Now imagine the "dirt" is our chemical reactant **A**, and the soap reacts with it. At any instant, the dirt-concentration inside the tub is *exactly the same* as the dirt-concentration in the water leaving the tub through the drain. **This single "exit concentration" tells us everything we need to know about how fast the reaction is occurring.**

By varying either the **inflow rate** or the **tank volume**, we change how long the molecules stay inside (the *residence time*). Shorter stays → less conversion → higher exit concentration. Longer stays → more conversion → lower exit concentration. **Plotting these data points mathematically reveals the reaction kinetics.**

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics Used in CSTR Kinetics

- **Volumetric Flow Rate (v₀):** measured in **m³/s** or **L/min**
- **Reactor Volume (V):** measured in **m³** or **L**
- **Space Time (τ = V/v₀):** measured in **seconds (s)**
- **Concentration (C_A):** measured in **mol/L** or **kmol/m³**
- **Reaction Rate (-r_A):** measured in **mol/(L·s)**
- **Temperature (T):** measured in **K** or **°C**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion (X)** is dimensionless and defined as $X = \dfrac{F_{A0} - F_A}{F_{A0}} = \dfrac{C_{A0} - C_A}{C_{A0}}$, assuming constant density.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle — Why a CSTR Works for Kinetics

A CSTR is ideal for kinetic studies because it **decouples the rate of reaction from position**. In a Plug Flow Reactor (PFR), composition changes continuously along the length, requiring integration. In a CSTR, the entire volume is at the outlet composition $C_A$, so the rate is *spatially uniform* and equals:

$$-r_A = \frac{F_{A0} \cdot X}{V} = \frac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{\tau}$$

This single expression is the **design equation** of a CSTR and the cornerstone of every kinetic calculation.

### 2.2 Step-Wise Logical Framework

1. **Assume** a rate law of the form $-r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$ (where *n* is unknown).
2. **Substitute** $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$ into the rate law.
3. **Apply** the steady-state mass balance: $V/v_0 = X / (-r_A)$.
4. **Rearrange** to obtain a linearised form suitable for plotting on graph paper.
5. **Plot** experimental data of τ vs X (or equivalent) and extract *k* and *n*.

### 2.3 Linearised Forms for Different Reaction Orders

| Reaction Order (n) | Rate Law | Rearranged CSTR Equation | Linearised Form (y = mx + c) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Zero** | $-r_A = k$ | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{k}$ | $\tau$ vs $X$ → straight line through origin; slope = $C_{A0}/k$ |
| **First** | $-r_A = k \cdot C_A$ | $\tau = \dfrac{X}{k(1-X)}$ | $\dfrac{\tau}{X}$ vs $\tau$ gives $1/k$; or $\ln\left(\dfrac{X}{1-X}\right)$ vs $\ln(k\tau)$ |
| **Second** | $-r_A = k \cdot C_A^2$ | $\tau = \dfrac{X}{k \cdot C_{A0}(1-X)^2}$ | $\dfrac{\tau(1-X)^2}{X}$ vs $C_{A0}$ gives $1/k$ |
| **n-th** | $-r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$ | $\tau \cdot k \cdot C_{A0}^{n-1} = \dfrac{X}{(1-X)^n}$ | $\ln(\tau)$ vs $\ln\left(\dfrac{X}{(1-X)^n}\right)$ gives *n* |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Typical Units | Engineering Utility |
|:-:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Space Time | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{v_0}$ | s (or min) | Average residence time; key experimental control knob |
| 2 | Space Velocity | $SV = \dfrac{v_0}{V} = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ | s⁻¹ (or min⁻¹) | Reciprocal of residence time; volumetric throughput per unit volume |
| 3 | Conversion | $X = \dfrac{C_{A0} - C_A}{C_{A0}}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of reactant A converted to products |
| 4 | CSTR Design Equation | $\dfrac{V}{F_{A0}} = \dfrac{X}{-r_A}$ | (L·s)/mol | Connects reactor geometry, throughput, conversion and kinetics |
| 5 | General Rate Law | $-r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$ | mol/(L·s) | Power-law form of intrinsic kinetics |
| 6 | First-Order Test | $k \cdot \tau = \dfrac{X}{1-X}$ | dimensionless | Check 1st-order: plot $\dfrac{X}{1-X}$ vs $\tau$ → straight line through origin |
| 7 | Second-Order Test | $k \cdot \tau \cdot C_{A0} = \dfrac{X}{(1-X)^2}$ | dimensionless | Check 2nd-order: plot $\dfrac{X}{(1-X)^2}$ vs $\tau$ → straight line through origin |
| 8 | Arrhenius Equation | $k = A \cdot e^{-E/(RT)}$ | varies with order | Temperature dependence of the rate constant |
| 9 | Eyring-Polanyi (alt.) | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k}{T}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_B}{h}\right) + \dfrac{\Delta S^{\ddagger}}{R} - \dfrac{\Delta H^{\ddagger}}{RT}$ | — | Transition-state theory alternative to Arrhenius |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The CSTR kinetic study forms the empirical basis for **industrial reactor design**, **scale-up** from lab to plant (since geometry-independent), **catalyst screening**, and **safety analysis** (predicting runaway behaviour in exothermic systems).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The General CSTR Mass Balance (Mole Balance on Reactant A)

**Step 1 — Write the general unsteady-state mole balance.**

For reactant *A* in any reactor:

$$\text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$

$$F_{A0} - F_A + G_A = \dfrac{dN_A}{dt}$$

where $G_A = r_A \cdot V$ is the rate of generation (negative for consumption).

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state condition.**

At steady state, accumulation is zero: $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = 0$.

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A \cdot V = 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange and isolate the reactor volume.**

$$r_A \cdot V = F_A - F_{A0}$$

$$V = \dfrac{F_A - F_{A0}}{r_A} = \dfrac{-F_{A0} X}{r_A} = \dfrac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to space-time form using $\tau = V/v_0$ and $F_{A0} = v_0 \cdot C_{A0}$.**

$$\tau = \dfrac{V}{v_0} = \dfrac{F_{A0} X}{v_0 \cdot (-r_A)} = \dfrac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{-r_A}$$

**Final CSTR Design Equation:**

$$\boxed{\;\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{-r_A}\;}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — First-Order Kinetics Linearisation

**Step 1 — Substitute the first-order rate law.**

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A = k \cdot C_{A0}(1-X)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the CSTR design equation.**

$$\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{k \cdot C_{A0}(1-X)} = \dfrac{X}{k(1-X)}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply to obtain the canonical form.**

$$k \cdot \tau = \dfrac{X}{1-X}$$

**Step 4 — Invert to extract k directly from data.**

$$k = \dfrac{X}{\tau (1-X)}$$

**Step 5 — Linearised form for graphical verification.**

Multiply both sides by $(1-X)$:

$$X = k\tau (1-X)$$

$$X = k\tau - k\tau X$$

$$X + k\tau X = k\tau$$

$$X(1 + k\tau) = k\tau$$

$$\dfrac{X}{1-X} = k\tau$$

Therefore, a plot of $\dfrac{X}{1-X}$ versus $\tau$ **must pass through the origin** with slope = *k* if the reaction is truly first order. Any deviation suggests either a different order or experimental error.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Second-Order Kinetics Linearisation

**Step 1 — Substitute the second-order rate law.**

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^2 = k \cdot C_{A0}^2 (1-X)^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the CSTR design equation.**

$$\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{k \cdot C_{A0}^2 (1-X)^2} = \dfrac{X}{k \cdot C_{A0}(1-X)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange.**

$$k \cdot C_{A0} \cdot \tau = \dfrac{X}{(1-X)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for k.**

$$k = \dfrac{X}{C_{A0} \cdot \tau \cdot (1-X)^2}$$

**Step 5 — For nth-order generalisation.**

For $-r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$, substituting $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$:

$$\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{k \cdot C_{A0}^n (1-X)^n} = \dfrac{X}{k \cdot C_{A0}^{n-1}(1-X)^n}$$

Taking natural logarithms:

$$\ln(\tau) = \ln(k) + (n-1)\ln(C_{A0}) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X}{(1-X)^n}\right)$$

If $C_{A0}$ is held constant across experiments, this becomes:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X}{(1-X)^n}\right) = \ln(k\tau) - (n-1)\ln(C_{A0})$$

A more general *trial-and-error* approach is to plot $\ln(\tau)$ vs $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X}{(1-X)^n}\right)$ for trial values of *n* until a straight line of slope 1 is obtained.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (First-Order Verification)

**Given experimental data from a CSTR at 35 °C:**

| Run | v₀ (L/min) | V (L) | C_A (mol/L) | C_A0 (mol/L) |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 0.50 | 2.0 | 0.380 | 1.000 |
| 2 | 1.00 | 2.0 | 0.500 | 1.000 |
| 3 | 1.50 | 2.0 | 0.600 | 1.000 |
| 4 | 2.00 | 2.0 | 0.667 | 1.000 |

**Step 1 — Compute space time for each run using $\tau = V/v_0$.**

| Run | τ (min) | X = 1 − C_A/C_A0 | X/(1−X) |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 4.00 | 0.620 | 1.632 |
| 2 | 2.00 | 0.500 | 1.000 |
| 3 | 1.33 | 0.400 | 0.667 |
| 4 | 1.00 | 0.333 | 0.500 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression of $\dfrac{X}{1-X}$ vs $\tau$.**

The straight-line fit through the origin gives:

$$\text{slope} = k = \dfrac{\sum \tau \cdot [X/(1-X)]}{\sum \tau^2}$$

Numerator: $4.00(1.632) + 2.00(1.000) + 1.33(0.667) + 1.00(0.500) = 6.528 + 2.000 + 0.887 + 0.500 = 9.915$

Denominator: $4.00^2 + 2.00^2 + 1.33^2 + 1.00^2 = 16.00 + 4.00 + 1.769 + 1.00 = 22.769$

$$k = \dfrac{9.915}{22.769} \approx 0.4355 \;\; \text{min}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

The high correlation coefficient (r² ≈ 1.0) and a clean linear plot through the origin confirm **first-order kinetics** with $k \approx 0.436 \;\; \text{min}^{-1}$ at 35 °C.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Reactor Types for Kinetic Studies

| Feature | CSTR (MFR) | PFR | Batch Reactor |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Composition uniformity** | Perfect (spatially uniform) | Gradual axial change | Time-varying only |
| **Rate expression evaluated at** | Outlet composition only | Multiple points (requires integration) | Any sampled time |
| **Data points per experiment** | One steady-state point | One axial profile | Time-series |
| **Ease of k,n determination** | Excellent (single algebraic eq.) | Moderate (requires integral) | Good (requires differentiation) |
| **Hold-up time control** | Direct via τ = V/v₀ | Indirect via v₀ | Direct (clock-time) |
| **Sensitivity to flow maldistribution** | Low | High | N/A |
| **Best suited for** | Constant-density liquid reactions | Gas-phase, plug-flow reactions | Slow reactions, multi-sample analysis |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CSTR Kinetic-Study Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeedSystem [FEED PREPARATION UNIT]
        A1[Reactant A Reservoir] --> B1[Rotameter / Flow Indicator]
        A2[Solvent / Diluent Reservoir] --> B1
        B1 --> C1[Feed Mixing Chamber]
    end
    subgraph ReactorUnit [CSTR REACTOR VESSEL]
        C1 --> D1[Inlet Port]
        D1 --> E1[Impeller Stirrer Motor]
        E1 --> F1[Reaction Mixture in Vessel]
        F1 --> G1[Temperature Probe]
        F1 --> H1[Overflow / Outlet Port]
    end
    subgraph SamplingUnit [PRODUCT ANALYSIS TRAIN]
        H1 --> I1[Sampling Valve]
        I1 --> J1[Quench / Neutralisation Bath]
        J1 --> K1[Titration / Spectrophotometer]
        K1 --> L1[Data Logger and PC]
    end
    L1 --> M1[Kinetic Parameter Calculator k and n]
    M1 --> N1[Validated Rate Law Report]
    style FeedSystem fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style ReactorUnit fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style SamplingUnit fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

### 4.2 Conceptual State-Flow of the Experimental Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Calibrate Feed Flow Rate] --> S2[Step 2: Charge Reactor with Reactant A]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Set Impeller RPM and Jacket Temperature]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Initiate Feed Flow and Start Clock]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Wait for 3 to 5 Space Times for Steady State]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Withdraw Outlet Sample]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Analyse Concentration Ca via Titration or Spectrophotometry]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Compute tau, X, and Rate ra]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Repeat at Different v0 to Vary tau]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Plot Linearised Forms and Determine k, n]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Repeat at Different T to Obtain Arrhenius Parameters]
    style S5 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style S10 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

### 4.3 Schematic Representation of Mass Balance in a CSTR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In1[Feed Stream F A0 mol per s] --> Sub[Mixing and Reaction Zone Uniform Composition Ca]
    Sub --> Out1[Exit Stream F A mol per s]
    Sub -.->|-ra times V| Rxn[R ate of Consumption of A per unit Volume]
    In1 --> Eq[Steady State Balance FA0 minus FA plus ra times V equals 0]
    Out1 --> Eq
    Rxn --> Eq
    style Sub fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style Eq fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree for Determining Reaction Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Plot X by 1 minus X vs tau linear through origin?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Order n equals 1]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Plot X by 1 minus X squared vs tau linear?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Order n equals 2]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Plot X vs tau linear through origin?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3[Order n equals 0 Zero Order]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4[Trial values of n from 0.5 to 3 to find best linear fit]
    Q4 --> A4[Most likely order is the value that maximises r squared]
    style A1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style A2 fill:#c8e6c9
    style A3 fill:#c8e6c9
    style A4 fill:#fff9c4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define space time and space velocity for a CSTR. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**
Space time $(\tau)$ is defined as the time required to process one reactor volume of feed under the given operating conditions. Mathematically:

$$\tau = \dfrac{V}{v_0} \;\; \text{(units: s or min)}$$

Space velocity (SV) is defined as the number of reactor volumes of feed processed per unit time at reaction conditions. Mathematically:

$$SV = \dfrac{v_0}{V} = \dfrac{1}{\tau} \;\; \text{(units: s}^{-1} \text{ or min}^{-1)}$$

The two are **reciprocals** of each other: $SV = 1/\tau$. Space time is the direct control variable in kinetic experiments because it links residence time to the achievable conversion.

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why is a CSTR preferred over a PFR for laboratory kinetic studies?**

**Model Answer:**
A CSTR is preferred because the entire reactor volume is at the **outlet composition**, so the reaction rate $(-r_A)$ is uniform throughout and can be computed directly from the algebraic CSTR design equation:

$$\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{-r_A}$$

This avoids the integral $\int_{0}^{X} \dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ required for a PFR. Furthermore, the reaction rate is determined at a single, well-defined composition, simplifying both data acquisition and the determination of kinetic parameters *k* and *n*. The CSTR also provides independent control of residence time via flow rate without disturbing reactor geometry.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** *Derive the design equation for a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor operating at steady state, starting from the general mole balance on reactant A.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the general mole balance on reactant *A*:

$$\text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A \cdot V = \dfrac{dN_A}{dt}$$

Applying steady state, $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = 0$:

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A \cdot V = 0$$

Since reactant *A* is consumed, $r_A = -(-r_A)$. Substituting $F_A = F_{A0}(1-X)$:

$$F_{A0} - F_{A0}(1-X) - (-r_A) \cdot V = 0$$

$$F_{A0} \cdot X = (-r_A) \cdot V$$

Recognising that $F_{A0} = v_0 \cdot C_{A0}$ and dividing both sides by $v_0 \cdot (-r_A)$:

$$\tau = \dfrac{V}{v_0} = \dfrac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{-r_A}$$

This is the **CSTR design equation**, valid for any rate law.

**(b)** *For a first-order liquid-phase reaction A → Products carried out in a CSTR at 35 °C, the following data are recorded. Determine the rate constant k using the linearised form. Given: C_A0 = 2.0 mol/L, V = 1.5 L.* **[7 Marks]**

| Run | v₀ (L/min) | C_A (mol/L) |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 0.30 | 0.80 |
| 2 | 0.50 | 1.00 |
| 3 | 0.75 | 1.20 |
| 4 | 1.00 | 1.333 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\tau = V/v_0$ for each run.**

| Run | τ (min) | X = 1 − C_A/C_A0 | 1 − X | X/(1−X) |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 5.000 | 0.600 | 0.400 | 1.500 |
| 2 | 3.000 | 0.500 | 0.500 | 1.000 |
| 3 | 2.000 | 0.400 | 0.600 | 0.667 |
| 4 | 1.500 | 0.333 | 0.667 | 0.500 |

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order linearised equation.**

$$k\tau = \dfrac{X}{1-X} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dfrac{X}{1-X} = k\tau$$

So a plot of $Y = \dfrac{X}{1-X}$ vs $\tau$ yields a straight line of slope *k* through the origin.

**Step 3 — Compute slope via least squares.**

$$\sum \tau \cdot Y = 5.000(1.500) + 3.000(1.000) + 2.000(0.667) + 1.500(0.500)$$
$$= 7.500 + 3.000 + 1.334 + 0.750 = 12.584$$

$$\sum \tau^2 = 25.00 + 9.00 + 4.00 + 2.25 = 40.25$$

$$k = \dfrac{\sum \tau Y}{\sum \tau^2} = \dfrac{12.584}{40.25} \approx 0.3126 \;\; \text{min}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with an individual point (Run 2):**

$$k = \dfrac{X}{\tau(1-X)} = \dfrac{0.500}{3.000 \times 0.500} = 0.333 \;\; \text{min}^{-1}$$

Both estimates are within ~6 %, confirming consistency. The recommended value is **k ≈ 0.31 min⁻¹** at 35 °C.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** *For the elementary reaction A + B → Products (second order, equimolar feed), derive the CSTR design equation in terms of space time and conversion.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

For a second-order reaction with equimolar feed ($C_{A0} = C_{B0}$), the rate law is:

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B = k \cdot C_A^2 \quad \text{(since C_A = C_B at all times)}$$

Using the definition of conversion:

$$C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$$

Substituting into the rate expression:

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_{A0}^2 (1-X)^2$$

Applying the CSTR design equation:

$$\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{-r_A} = \dfrac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{k \cdot C_{A0}^2 (1-X)^2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\tau = \dfrac{X}{k \cdot C_{A0} (1-X)^2}\;}$$

Rearranging for the rate constant:

$$k = \dfrac{X}{\tau \cdot C_{A0} (1-X)^2}$$

This is the canonical equation used in the laboratory to verify second-order behaviour: a plot of $\dfrac{X}{(1-X)^2}$ vs $\tau$ must be linear with slope $k \cdot C_{A0}$ through the origin.

**(b)** *In a CSTR study of the saponification of ethyl acetate (second-order), the following data are obtained at 30 °C. Determine k. Given: C_A0 = 0.05 mol/L, V = 0.8 L.* **[7 Marks]**

| Run | v₀ (mL/min) | C_A at outlet (mol/L) |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 50 | 0.020 |
| 2 | 80 | 0.025 |
| 3 | 120 | 0.030 |
| 4 | 180 | 0.035 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert V and v₀ to consistent units.**

Use V = 0.8 L = 800 mL.

| Run | τ = V/v₀ (min) | X = 1 − C_A/C_A0 | 1−X | X/(1−X)² |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 16.00 | 0.600 | 0.400 | 3.750 |
| 2 | 10.00 | 0.500 | 0.500 | 2.000 |
| 3 | 6.667 | 0.400 | 0.600 | 1.111 |
| 4 | 4.444 | 0.300 | 0.700 | 0.612 |

**Step 2 — Apply the second-order CSTR equation.**

$$k = \dfrac{X}{\tau \cdot C_{A0} \cdot (1-X)^2}$$

Equivalently, slope of $\dfrac{X}{(1-X)^2}$ vs $\tau$ equals $k \cdot C_{A0}$.

**Step 3 — Compute slope via least squares.**

$$\sum \tau \cdot \left[\dfrac{X}{(1-X)^2}\right] = 16.00(3.750) + 10.00(2.000) + 6.667(1.111) + 4.444(0.612)$$
$$= 60.000 + 20.000 + 7.408 + 2.720 = 90.128$$

$$\sum \tau^2 = 256.00 + 100.00 + 44.448 + 19.753 = 420.20$$

$$\text{slope} = \dfrac{90.128}{420.20} = 0.2145 \;\; \text{min}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for k.**

$$k \cdot C_{A0} = 0.2145 \;\; \Rightarrow\;\; k = \dfrac{0.2145}{0.050} = 4.29 \;\; \text{L/(mol·min)}$$

**Step 5 — Verification with Run 1:**

$$k = \dfrac{0.600}{16.00 \times 0.050 \times 0.160} = \dfrac{0.600}{0.128} = 4.69 \;\; \text{L/(mol·min)}$$

The values are reasonably consistent. **Average k ≈ 4.5 L/(mol·min) at 30 °C.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap (–2 marks):** Students frequently plug τ in *seconds* while concentration is in *mol/L* and *k* is expected in *L/(mol·min)*. Always normalise units **before** computation.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $C_{A0}$ (–2 marks):** In the second-order case, students write $k\tau = X/(1-X)^2$ but forget the $C_{A0}$ multiplier, off by a factor of $C_{A0}$ in the final answer.
> 3. **Plotting without going through the origin (–1 mark):** The CSTR linearised forms *must* pass through the origin; if intercept is non-zero, the assumed order is wrong.
> 4. **Steady-state violation (–3 marks):** Sampling the outlet before 3–5 space times have elapsed gives transient data, leading to non-linear plots and incorrect *k*. Always state the steady-state confirmation step.
> 5. **Constant-density assumption (–1 mark):** The simple $X = (C_{A0} - C_A)/C_{A0}$ form requires constant density; for gaseous or high-conversion liquid systems use molar flow expressions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ The **CSTR design equation** $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{-r_A}$ is the *single most important* formula for this experiment.
- ✅ At steady state, **outlet concentration = concentration everywhere inside the reactor**.
- ✅ **Space time** $\tau = V/v_0$ is controlled experimentally by changing $v_0$ (flow rate).
- ✅ **Space velocity** $SV = 1/\tau$; never confuse the two in viva voce.
- ✅ For **first-order** verification: plot $\dfrac{X}{1-X}$ vs $\tau$ → straight line through origin, slope = *k*.
- ✅ For **second-order** verification: plot $\dfrac{X}{(1-X)^2}$ vs $\tau$ → straight line through origin, slope = $k \cdot C_{A0}$.
- ✅ For **zero-order** verification: plot $X$ vs $\tau$ → straight line through origin, slope = $k/C_{A0}$.
- ✅ **Reaction order (n)** is determined by *trial*: assume *n*, plot $\ln(\tau)$ vs $\ln[X/(1-X)^n]$; the value of *n* giving slope ≈ 1 is the correct order.
- ✅ **Arrhenius parameters** (A and E) are obtained by repeating the experiment at multiple temperatures and plotting $\ln(k)$ vs $1/T$.
- ✅ Always wait **at least 3–5 residence times** after changing flow rate before sampling, to ensure true steady state.
- ✅ Maintain **isothermal conditions** via a thermostatted jacket; otherwise intrinsic kinetics cannot be isolated from thermal effects.
- ✅ **Impeller RPM** must be high enough to ensure perfect mixing (verified by tracer-response or by sampling at multiple points).
- ✅ Validate *k* and *n* by comparing **experimental τ vs X** with the **theoretical curve** predicted from the design equation.
- ✅ For the **KTU lab record**, always include: aim, apparatus, chemicals, procedure, observation table, sample calculation, graph, result, and sources of error.
- ✅ Common reactions used in the KTU lab: **saponification of ethyl acetate** (2nd order) and **inversion of sucrose** (pseudo-1st order).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinetic Studies in an Isothermal Tubular Reactor
Kinetic Studies in a Plug Flow Reactor
Kinetic Studies in a Packed Bed Reactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL607 - REACTION ENGINEERING AND PROCESS CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Kinetic Studies in a Packed Bed Reactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Packed Bed Reactor (PBR)** is a tubular heterogeneous catalytic reactor in which a continuous fluid phase (gas or liquid) flows through a *stationary* bed of solid catalyst particles. The fluid enters from the top (or bottom) and reacts at the internal active sites of the catalyst as it traverses the length of the reactor. Unlike a fluidized bed, the catalyst particles remain *immobilized* within the column, maintaining a defined spatial arrangement and contact pattern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBBL607):** *A Packed Bed Reactor (PBR) is a cylindrical vessel uniformly packed with a stationary catalyst bed, used to determine intrinsic reaction kinetics for heterogeneous catalytic reactions by analyzing inlet and outlet concentrations as a function of space velocity, temperature, and pressure drop across the bed.*

The kinetic study in a PBR is performed by measuring the conversion of reactant (or formation of product) as a function of operating parameters such as **weight of catalyst (W)**, **molar flow rate (F_A0)**, and **temperature (T)**. The integral and differential methods of analysis are then applied to the conversion data to obtain the rate law parameters (rate constant *k* and reaction order *n*).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Marble Tower Water Filter"**
>
> Imagine a transparent vertical pipe filled with marbles (catalyst particles), and water containing dissolved dye (reactant) is poured in from the top. As the colored water trickles down through the gaps between marbles, the dye molecules adsorb onto the marble surfaces, get chemically transformed (reacted), and the cleaned water (product) emerges from the bottom.
>
> The **marbles** represent the catalyst, the **dye-laden water** represents the reactant feed, and the **clean water** is the product. The slower the flow, the more time the dye spends in contact with the marbles — this is exactly analogous to a higher **space time** in a PBR leading to higher **conversion**.

This analogy helps visualize three critical PBR concepts:
- **Plug Flow Assumption:** Just as every drop of water at the top moves downward in *plug-like* fashion without back-mixing, fluid in a PBR is assumed to flow with negligible axial dispersion.
- **Catalyst Contact:** The reactant contacts the catalyst externally (film diffusion) and internally (pore diffusion) before reacting at active sites.
- **Pressure Drop:** The marbles resist the water flow; similarly, catalyst particles create a measurable **pressure drop (ΔP)** along the bed length.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard Temperature | T₀ | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| Standard Pressure | P₀ | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** |
| Mears Criterion Constant | M | Dimensionless (M ≤ 0.15) |
| Bed Porosity | ε | **0.35 – 0.50** (typical) |
| Particle Sphericity | φₛ | **0.6 – 0.95** (typical) |
| Catalyst Bulk Density | ρ_b | **500 – 1500 kg/m³** |
| Gas Constant for Pressure | R | **8.314 × 10⁻³ kJ/(mol·K)** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — Structured Logic Breakdown

A kinetic study in a PBR revolves around measuring how conversion (**X**) changes as the operating conditions are varied. The following structured logic explains the "why" and "how" behind each experimental step:

- **Why is the catalyst held stationary?**
  A packed bed is chosen over a CSTR or fluidized bed when **intrinsic kinetic parameters** need to be measured without catalyst entrainment. Stationary packing allows precise correlation of conversion with **W/F_A0** (the modified space time).

- **How is conversion measured experimentally?**
  A feed of known concentration (C_A0) is admitted at a controlled flow rate. At the reactor outlet, the residual concentration (C_A) is measured (typically via **gas chromatography, IR spectroscopy, or titration**). Conversion is calculated as:

  $$X_A = \frac{C_{A0} - C_A}{C_{A0}}$$

- **Why do we vary the flow rate (or W/F_A0)?**
  By changing the feed flow rate while keeping the catalyst mass constant, the **space time** is varied. Plotting X vs. W/F_A0 gives the **integral reactor data** needed to determine the rate law.

- **How is the rate law obtained from data?**
  Two classical methods are used:
  1. **Integral Method:** Assume a rate law (e.g., –r_A = k·C_Aⁿ), integrate the design equation, and check the fit.
  2. **Differential Method:** Plot conversion vs. position; take numerical derivatives to extract dX/d(W/F_A0), which directly equals –r_A.

- **How is pressure drop handled?**
  For gas-phase reactions, the pressure drop can be significant. The **Ergun equation** quantifies ΔP, and the resulting pressure profile must be coupled with the design equation for accurate kinetic evaluation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** expected in KTU ESE questions on PBR kinetics. Mastering these is essential for full marks.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dX}{d(W/F_{A0})} = -r_A$ | Differential PBR design equation | mol/(g_cat·s) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{W}{F_{A0}} = \displaystyle\int_0^{X} \dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ | Integral PBR design equation | g_cat·s/mol |
| 3 | $X_A = \dfrac{F_{A0} - F_A}{F_{A0}}$ | Conversion in terms of molar flow | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$ | Molar flow vs. conversion | mol/s |
| 5 | $\tau' = \dfrac{W}{F_{A0}}$ | Modified space time (weight-time) | g_cat·s/mol |
| 6 | $-r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$ | General power-law rate expression | mol/(g_cat·s) |
| 7 | $C_A = C_{A0}\dfrac{(1-X)}{1 + \varepsilon X}\dfrac{P}{P_0}\dfrac{T_0}{T}$ | Gas-phase concentration (variable density) | mol/m³ |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\dfrac{G}{\rho}\dfrac{(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3}\left[\dfrac{150\mu(1-\varepsilon)}{D_p} + 1.75 G\right]\dfrac{1}{D_p}$ | **Ergun equation** (differential form) | Pa/m |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150\mu(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3}\dfrac{u_s}{D_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75(1-\varepsilon)\rho u_s^2}{\varepsilon^3 D_p}$ | **Ergun equation** (compact form) | Pa/m |
| 10 | $\dfrac{M_t - r_A \rho_b R n}{k_c C_{A_b}} < 0.15$ | **Mears criterion** (external diffusion) | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{-r_{A,\text{actual}}}{-r_{A,\text{at surface}}}$ | Catalyst effectiveness factor | 0 < η ≤ 1 |
| 12 | $N_{Re,p} = \dfrac{D_p G}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number at particle level | Dimensionless |
| 13 | $N_{Sc} = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}}$ | Schmidt number | Dimensionless |
| 14 | $J_D = \dfrac{k_c}{G} N_{Sc}^{2/3}$ | Mass-transfer correlation (Chilton–Colburn) | Dimensionless |
| 15 | $\phi = R\sqrt{\dfrac{k \rho_p S_a}{D_e}}$ | Thiele modulus | Dimensionless |
| 16 | $\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi}\left[\coth(\phi) - \dfrac{1}{\phi}\right]$ | Spherical pellet effectiveness factor | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on KTU Convention:** All rate equations use catalyst mass (W in grams or kg) as the independent variable, *not* reactor volume. This is the hallmark difference between PBR and PFR formulations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Packed bed reactors are the **workhorse of the chemical industry**, particularly wherever heterogeneous catalysis is required:

- **Petrochemical Industry:** Catalytic cracking, hydrodesulfurization, and catalytic reforming in petroleum refining.
- **Ammonia Synthesis:** The Haber–Bosch process uses a multi-bed PBR with iron catalyst at 400–500 °C and 150–300 atm.
- **Environmental Catalysis:** Automotive catalytic converters, DeNOx units in power plants, and VOC abatement in industrial exhaust.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Fixed-bed hydrogenation and oxidation reactors.
- **Polymerization:** Ziegler–Natta catalyzed polymerization of polyolefins in slurry/gas-phase PBRs.

Accurate kinetic studies in PBRs are essential for **scale-up**, **reactor sizing**, and **economic optimization** of these processes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Differential PBR Design Equation

We start from a differential mass balance on reactant A over a thin slice of catalyst bed of mass **dW**.

**Step 1 — State the conservation principle.**
The molar rate of A entering the slice minus the molar rate of A leaving the slice equals the rate of disappearance of A by reaction within the slice.

$$F_A \big\vert_W - F_A \big\vert_{W+dW} + r_A \cdot dW = 0$$

**Step 2 — Convert the difference to a derivative.**
The negative sign indicates that F_A decreases as W increases (the reactant is consumed).

$$\frac{dF_A}{dW} = r_A$$

**Step 3 — Introduce conversion X_A.**
By definition, the molar flow rate of A is related to inlet conditions and conversion:
