```mermaid
    SPnode[Setpoint SP] --> SumNode[Summing Junction]
    PVfb[Process Variable PV] --> SumNode
    SumNode --> ErrSig[Error Signal e t]
    ErrSig --> PIDctl[PID Controller]
    PIDctl --> CtrlOut[Controller Output OP percent]
    CtrlOut --> SCReg[SCR Power Modulator]
    SCReg --> Heater[Heater Element]
    Heater --> ProcTnk[Process Tank Air or Water]
    ProcTnk --> RTDsens[Pt-100 RTD Sensor]
    RTDsens --> SgCond[Signal Conditioner]
    SgCond --> PVfb
    ProcTnk --> FanLoad[Disturbance Cooling Fan]
    ProcTnk --> RecOut[Recorder DAQ]
```

**Interpretation of Block Diagram:**

- The **summing junction** algebraically subtracts the feedback PV from the SP to generate the error.
- The **PID controller** processes this error through three parallel paths: proportional, integral, derivative.
- The **controller output (OP)** is a percentage (0–100 %) that commands the SCR.
- The **SCR module** phase-angle-fires the AC mains to deliver proportional RMS voltage to the heater.
- The **process tank** acts as the plant $G_p(s)$.
- The **RTD sensor + signal conditioner** is the measurement transfer function $H(s)$ (assumed unity).
- The **fan** injects an external disturbance $D(s)$ used to test **regulatory performance**.

---

### 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartNode([Start Lab Session]) --> CalibStep[Calibrate RTD Against Reference Thermometer]
    CalibStep --> OLStep[Open Loop Step Test]
    OLStep --> ReadCurve[Record Step Response Curve]
    ReadCurve --> FitFOPDT[Determine Kp Tau Theta from Curve]
    FitFOPDT --> TuneZN[Apply Ziegler Nichols Tuning]
    TuneZN --> CLPTest[Close Loop P Controller Test]
    CLPTest --> CLPITest[Close Loop PI Controller Test]
    CLPITest --> CLPIDTest[Close Loop PID Controller Test]
    CLPIDTest --> DistStep[Apply Step Disturbance via Fan]
    DistStep --> PerfEval[Compute IAE ISE ITAE and Plot]
    PerfEval --> ReportStep[Prepare Lab Report with Discussion]
    ReportStep --> EndNode([Session End])
```

**Operational Notes:**

- At each closed-loop test, record the **setpoint tracking** (servo response) and the **disturbance rejection** (regulatory response) separately.
- Save the time-stamped data in a CSV file via the DAQ software.
- Compare the **rise time, peak overshoot, settling time (±2 % band), and steady-state error** across P, PI, and PID.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Process Gain ($K_p$), Time Constant ($\tau$), and Dead Time ($\theta$) as applied to a temperature control trainer. State their units.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Process Gain ($K_p$):** The ratio of the change in steady-state process variable to the change in manipulated variable that caused it. For the temperature trainer, the unit is **°C per % heater input**.
- **Time Constant ($\tau$):** The time required for the process variable to reach **63.2 %** of its total change following a step input, measured in **seconds (s)** or **minutes (min)**.
- **Dead Time ($\theta$):** The time delay between the application of a step change in MV and the first observable change in PV, measured in **seconds (s)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
*What is "offset" in a proportional controller, and why does adding an integral term eliminate it?*

**Model Answer:**
- **Offset** is the **persistent steady-state error** that remains between the setpoint and the process variable when only a pure proportional (P) controller is used.
- It arises because a P controller requires a *non-zero error* to generate the controller output that balances the load.
- The **integral term** continuously integrates the error over time. As long as any error exists, the integral keeps increasing the controller output until the error is driven to zero, thereby **eliminating the offset**.
- Mathematically, at steady state, $\dfrac{d(\int e \, dt)}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow e = 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost**
> - Students often forget to **subtract the dead time** when computing $\tau$ from the step response ($t_{63.2\%} = \tau + \theta$, NOT $\tau$ alone).
> - Many write the FOPDT transfer function with the wrong sign convention for the exponential.
> - In tuning questions, marks are lost if **units** of $K_c$ are not stated explicitly (e.g., $\%\!/\!^\circ\text{C}$).
> - The **Ziegler-Nichols PI formula** is misremembered as $0.9\tau/(K_p \cdot \tau_I)$ — it is $\tau_I$ that you *compute*, not a divisor.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module Experiment 2]**

*(a)* With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the construction and working of a temperature control trainer used in the Reaction Engineering and Process Control Lab. Identify each block's physical hardware equivalent. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* An open-loop step test on a temperature trainer gave the following observations:
- Initial PV = 35 °C, Heater stepped from 20 % to 35 %
- Final steady-state PV = 85 °C
- PV first began to respond at $t = 25$ s
- PV reached 63.2 % of its total excursion at $t = 265$ s

Calculate $K_p$, $\tau$, and $\theta$, then determine **Ziegler-Nichols PI tuning parameters**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working — 7 Marks:**

The trainer comprises a **closed-loop feedback system** with the following hardware-mapped blocks (refer to Section 4.1 for the diagram):

| Block | Hardware |
|---|---|
| Setpoint generator | Digital thumbwheel / PC input |
| Summing junction | Difference amplifier / software subtraction |
| Controller | Digital PID (PC or stand-alone unit) |
| Final control element | SCR / Triac firing circuit |
| Process | Insulated tank + heater coil |
| Sensor | Pt-100 RTD |
| Transmitter | Wheatstone bridge + amplifier (0–5 V) |
| Recorder | DAQ card + PC |

**Working:** The user enters a desired temperature (SP). The RTD measures the actual PV. The error $e(t) = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$ is fed to the PID controller. The controller computes a corrective output (0–100 %) which fires the SCR. The SCR modulates the heater power. The tank temperature rises/falls accordingly. Feedback closes the loop.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Process Gain:**

$$K_p = \frac{\Delta \text{PV}}{\Delta \text{MV}} = \frac{85 - 35}{35 - 20} = \frac{50}{15} = 3.33 \; ^\circ\text{C} / \%$$

**Step 2 — Dead Time:**

$$\theta = 25 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Time Constant:**

$$\tau = t_{63.2\%} - \theta = 265 - 25 = 240 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — PI Controller Gains (Ziegler-Nichols):**

$$K_c = \frac{0.9 \, \tau}{K_p \, \theta} = \frac{0.9 \times 240}{3.33 \times 25} = \frac{216}{83.25} \approx 2.59 \; \% / ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\tau_I = 3.0 \, \theta = 3.0 \times 25 = 75 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — FOPDT Transfer Function:**

$$G_p(s) = \frac{3.33 \, e^{-25s}}{240 s + 1}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module Experiment 2 Alternative]**

*(a)* Compare the performance of **P, PI, and PID** controllers when applied to the temperature control trainer. Tabulate the response characteristics (rise time, overshoot, settling time, steady-state error) you would expect for each. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Describe the **Ziegler-Nichols open-loop tuning procedure** step-by-step. A process has $K_p = 4.0 \; ^\circ\text{C}/\%$, $\tau = 180$ s, $\theta = 20$ s. Calculate the PID tuning parameters using this method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table — 7 Marks:**

| Characteristic | P | PI | PID |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rise Time** | Moderate | Slightly higher | Fastest |
| **Peak Overshoot** | High (~30–50 %) | Moderate (~10–25 %) | Lowest (~5–10 %) |
| **Settling Time** | Long | Moderate | Shortest |
| **Steady-State Error** | Non-zero (offset) | Zero | Zero |
| **Disturbance Rejection** | Poor | Good | Best |
| **Stability Margin** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |

**Explanation:** Adding **Integral (I)** removes offset but introduces phase lag, slightly increasing overshoot. Adding **Derivative (D)** anticipates future error, providing a damping action that reduces overshoot and improves settling time.

---

**(b) Tuning Procedure — 7 Marks:**

**Step-by-Step Ziegler-Nichols Open-Loop Procedure:**

1. Place the controller in **manual mode**.
2. Allow the PV to reach a steady state at a nominal MV (e.g., 30 % heater).
3. Apply a small **step change** $\Delta u$ (typically 5–10 %).
4. Record the PV response curve until it reaches a new steady state.
5. From the curve, determine:
   - $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta \text{PV}_{ss}}{\Delta u}$
   - $\theta$ = time before PV starts changing
   - $\tau = t_{63.2\%} - \theta$
6. Apply the Z-N table values for the desired controller type.

**Numerical Computation for Given Values:**

Given $K_p = 4.0$, $\tau = 180$ s, $\theta = 20$ s, the Z-N formulas for PID are:

$$K_c = \frac{1.2 \, \tau}{K_p \, \theta} = \frac{1.2 \times 180}{4.0 \times 20} = \frac{216}{80} = 2.70 \; \% / ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\tau_I = 2.0 \, \theta = 2.0 \times 20 = 40 \; \text{s}$$

$$\tau_D = 0.5 \, \theta = 0.5 \times 20 = 10 \; \text{s}$$

The resulting closed-loop PID controller is therefore:

$$G_c(s) = 2.70 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{40 s} + 10 s \right)$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Temperature Control Trainer**

- ✅ The trainer is a **single-loop feedback control system** with temperature as the controlled variable.
- ✅ The hardware comprises **RTD sensor → Signal conditioner → PID controller → SCR/Heater → Process tank**.
- ✅ The mathematical model is the **FOPDT transfer function**: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p \, e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$.
- ✅ **Process Gain $K_p$** has units °C / % heater input.
- ✅ **Time constant $\tau$** = $t_{63.2\%} - \theta$, not $t_{63.2\%}$ alone.
- ✅ **Dead time $\theta$** arises from sensor lag, heat transfer delay, and signal transmission.
- ✅ **P controller** causes offset; **PI eliminates offset**; **PID reduces overshoot and settling time**.
- ✅ **Ziegler-Nichols open-loop tuning** is the KTU-preferred method; the formulas are:
  - PI: $K_c = 0.9\tau/(K_p\theta)$, $\tau_I = 3\theta$
  - PID: $K_c = 1.2\tau/(K_p\theta)$, $\tau_I = 2\theta$, $\tau_D = 0.5\theta$
- ✅ **Performance metrics**: IAE (most common), ISE, ITAE — all must be computed for grading.
- ✅ Always verify **closed-loop stability** by ensuring $K_c \le 0.5 \, K_u$.
- ✅ The **fan disturbance** is used to evaluate regulatory performance post-tuning.
- ✅ Typical lab observations include rise time, overshoot, settling time, and steady-state error — all plotted versus time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PID Controller Trainer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL607 - REACTION ENGINEERING AND PROCESS CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** PID Controller Trainer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) Controller Trainer** is a laboratory-scale apparatus used to study the dynamic behaviour of a closed-loop feedback control system. It physically implements the classical three-mode controller expressed in the **ideal parallel form**:

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(t)\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

Where each term carries a distinct engineering meaning:
- **$K_c$** — Proportional gain (dimensionless or %/% depending on transmitter calibration)
- **$\tau_I$** — Integral (reset) time in **minutes/repeat**
- **$\tau_D$** — Derivative (rate) time in **minutes**
- **$e(t)$** — Error signal: $e(t) = SP - PV$
- **$u(t)$** — Controller output (manipulated variable)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment trains students to identify the *First Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT)* model parameters from an open-loop step test, tune a PID controller using the **Ziegler–Nichols** method, and experimentally validate the closed-loop servo and regulatory responses.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **driving a car on a highway at 80 km/h with cruise control engaged**:

- **Proportional (P):** If the speed drifts to 78 km/h, you press the accelerator *harder* in proportion to how far you are from 80. The bigger the deviation, the harder you press. But P alone can leave you cruising at 79 km/h — a small **steady-state offset** always remains.
- **Integral (I):** Your brain accumulates that "1 km/h error over time" and continuously increases accelerator pressure until the offset is **completely eliminated**. The longer the error persists, the more aggressive your correction becomes.
- **Derivative (D):** You also anticipate. If you see the road sloping downhill ahead, you ease off the accelerator *before* the speed has actually risen. Derivative acts on the **rate of change** of error — it is the "look-ahead" or damping action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cruise Control as the Master Analogy:** This P + I + D combination is exactly how a process controller regulates temperature, pressure, flow, level, and composition in a chemical plant.

## 1.3 Purpose of the Trainer Kit

The trainer typically consists of:
- A **simulated process** (electronic analogue of a first-order or second-order plant)
- A **PID controller hardware module** (analog circuits or digital implementation)
- A **set-point generator** (step / ramp / sinusoidal input)
- **Indicators and recorders** (analog meters, CRO, or PC-based data acquisition)
- **Auto / Manual selector** and **bumpless transfer** switches

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the PID Algorithm

The controller output can be decomposed into three independent contributions:

$$u(t) = u_P(t) + u_I(t) + u_D(t)$$

### (a) Proportional Action — The "Present"
- **Output:** $u_P(t) = K_c \cdot e(t)$
- **Laplace transfer function:** $\dfrac{U_P(s)}{E(s)} = K_c$
- **Effect:** Reduces rise time, increases loop speed, but produces **steady-state offset**.
- **Why $K_c$ cannot be infinite:** Increasing $K_c$ too much causes oscillation and eventual instability (sustained or growing oscillations).

### (b) Integral Action — The "Past"
- **Output:** $u_I(t) = \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I}\displaystyle\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau$
- **Laplace transfer function:** $\dfrac{U_I(s)}{E(s)} = \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I s}$
- **Effect:** Eliminates steady-state offset (offsets can only exist if **$e(t) = 0$ over time**; integral forces this).
- **Reset Time $\tau_I$:** Time required for the integral contribution to equal the proportional contribution for a unit step error. Lower $\tau_I$ → faster reset.

### (c) Derivative Action — The "Future"
- **Output:** $u_D(t) = K_c \tau_D \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$
- **Laplace transfer function:** $\dfrac{U_D(s)}{E(s)} = K_c \tau_D s$
- **Effect:** Adds damping, reduces overshoot, anticipates future error.
- **Caveat:** Derivative cannot act on a constant error, hence it is **never used alone**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Transfer Function (Ideal Parallel Form):**
> $$G_c(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right] = K_c \cdot \dfrac{\tau_I \tau_D s^2 + \tau_I s + 1}{\tau_I s}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Equation / Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| PID Output | $u(t) = K_c\!\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}\right]$ | % | $0\% \le u(t) \le 100\%$ |
| Error | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | % of span | — |
| Closed-loop Char. Eq. | $1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)H(s) = 0$ | — | All roots in LHP → stable |
| Steady-state error (P-only) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{R}{1 + K_p K_c}$ | % | Non-zero when $\tau_I = \infty$ |
| Steady-state error (PI/PID) | $e_{ss} = 0$ for step input | % | Zero when $\tau_I$ finite |
| Rise Time | $t_r$ = time to go from 10% to 90% | s | Smaller is better |
| Settling Time (2% band) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | s | Process-dependent |
| Peak Overshoot | $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | % | $0 \le \zeta < 1$ |
| ISE Criterion | $J = \int_0^\infty [e(t)]^2\,dt$ | — | Minimized by tuning |
| ITAE Criterion | $J = \int_0^\infty t\,\vert e(t)\vert\,dt$ | — | Preferred for set-point |

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ written as `\vert \cdot \vert` (NOT as `|…|`) anywhere inside markdown tables, to prevent the table parser from breaking.

## 2.3 Tuning Methods at a Glance

### (a) Ziegler–Nichols Open-Loop (Reaction Curve) Method
Apply a small step $\Delta u$ in manual mode. Record the **FOPDT parameters** from the S-shaped response:
- $K_p$ = process gain
- $t_0$ (or $L$) = dead time
- $\tau$ (or $T$) = process time constant

### (b) Ziegler–Nichols Closed-Loop (Ultimate Cycle) Method
Set $\tau_I = \infty$ and $\tau_D = 0$. Increase $K_c$ until the loop oscillates with **constant amplitude**. Record:
- $K_u$ = ultimate gain
- $P_u$ = ultimate period

### (c) Cohen–Coon Method
A refinement of the open-loop Z-N method that gives faster disturbance rejection. Requires the same $K_p, t_0, \tau$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

PID controllers are the **workhorses of the chemical process industries (CPI)** — it is estimated that more than **95% of all regulatory control loops** in refineries, petrochemical plants, ammonia plants, and pharmaceutical units use some form of PID. The trainer teaches the engineer how to tune these loops for **stable, fast, and offset-free** operation.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal PID Algorithm from Control Objectives

**Step 1:** Define the instantaneous deviation from the desired operating point:
$$e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$$

**Step 2:** Decompose the corrective action into three orthogonal corrective components based on **time-domain behaviour**:
- A term proportional to the **magnitude** of error.
- A term proportional to the **accumulated history** of error.
- A term proportional to the **predicted future** of error (via current rate).

**Step 3:** Assign weighting constants $K_P, K_I, K_D$:
$$u(t) = K_P e(t) + K_I \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_D \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to the **controller-tuning convention** by writing $K_I = K_c / \tau_I$ and $K_D = K_c \tau_D$:

$$\boxed{u(t) = K_c e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt}}$$

This is the **standard parallel form** used in every commercial DCS / PLC.

## 3.2 Ziegler–Nichols Open-Loop Tuning — Full Derivation

**Step 1 — Open-Loop Step Test.** Put the controller in **MANUAL**. Apply a small step change $\Delta u$ in controller output. Wait for the process variable $y(t)$ to reach a new steady state.

**Step 2 — Read the FOPDT parameters.** Model the process as:
$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-t_0 s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

The graphical readings are:
$$K_p = \frac{\Delta y_{ss}}{\Delta u} \quad ; \quad t_0 = \text{dead time} \quad ; \quad \tau = \text{process time constant}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the tuning recipe (Z-N open loop, quarter-decay ratio):**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \frac{\tau}{K_p \, t_0} \\
\tau_I &= t_0 \\
\tau_D &= \frac{t_0}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example.** Suppose from the reaction curve: $K_p = 1.8$, $t_0 = 1.2\,\text{min}$, $\tau = 5.4\,\text{min}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \frac{5.4}{(1.8)(1.2)} = \frac{5.4}{2.16} = 2.5 \\
\tau_I &= 1.2\,\text{min} \\
\tau_D &= \frac{1.2}{2} = 0.6\,\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

These values produce a **quarter-decay response** (each successive peak is one-quarter of the previous).

## 3.3 Ziegler–Nichols Closed-Loop (Ultimate Cycle) — Full Derivation

**Step 1:** Set $\tau_I = \infty$, $\tau_D = 0$. Engage **AUTO** mode with $K_c$ very small.

**Step 2:** Gradually increase $K_c$ in small steps. After each step, perturb the set point slightly and observe the response.

**Step 3:** Stop increasing when the loop oscillates at **constant amplitude** (sustained oscillation, neither growing nor decaying). Record:
- $K_u$ = the gain at which this occurs
- $P_u$ = period of the oscillation

**Step 4 — Z-N Closed-Loop Recipe (PID controller):**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= 0.60 \, K_u \\
\tau_I &= 0.50 \, P_u \\
\tau_D &= 0.125 \, P_u
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example.** If $K_u = 8.4$ and $P_u = 2.8\,\text{min}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= (0.60)(8.4) = 5.04 \\
\tau_I &= (0.50)(2.8) = 1.4\,\text{min} \\
\tau_D &= (0.125)(2.8) = 0.35\,\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Cohen–Coon Tuning Formulas (Derivation Sketched)

Cohen–Coon uses the **ratio $t_0 / \tau$** as a primary tuning knob. The full expressions for a PID controller are:

$$K_c = \frac{\tau}{K_p t_0} \left[ \frac{4}{3} + \frac{t_0}{4\tau} \right]$$

$$\tau_I = t_0 \, \frac{32 + 6\,t_0/\tau}{13 + 8\,t_0/\tau}$$

$$\tau_D = t_0 \, \frac{4}{11 + 2\,t_0/\tau}$$

**Numerical Example** (same FOPDT: $K_p = 1.8$, $t_0 = 1.2$, $\tau = 5.4$):

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \frac{5.4}{(1.8)(1.2)}\left[\frac{4}{3} + \frac{1.2}{(4)(5.4)}\right] = 2.5 \times \left[1.333 + 0.0556\right] = 2.5 \times 1.389 = 3.47 \\
\tau_I &= (1.2)\,\frac{32 + 6(1.2/5.4)}{13 + 8(1.2/5.4)} = 1.2 \times \frac{32 + 1.333}{13 + 1.778} = 1.2 \times \frac{33.333}{14.778} = 2.71\,\text{min} \\
\tau_D &= (1.2)\,\frac{4}{11 + 2(1.2/5.4)} = 1.2 \times \frac{4}{11.444} = 0.42\,\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison Insight:** Cohen–Coon predicts a **higher $K_c$** than Z-N open loop → faster disturbance rejection, but slightly more aggressive and slightly more overshoot.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Controller Action

| Tuning Change | Rise Time $t_r$ | Overshoot $M_p$ | Settling Time $t_s$ | Steady-State Error $e_{ss}$ | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $K_c$ | Decreases | Increases | Minimum near critical $K_c$ | Decreases (P-only) | Decreases |
| Decrease $\tau_I$ (faster I) | Decreases | Increases | Decreases | Eliminated faster | Decreases |
| Increase $\tau_D$ | Minor | Decreases strongly | Decreases | No effect | Increases (more damping) |
| Decrease $\tau_D$ | — | — | — | — | More oscillatory |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Block Diagram of a PID-Controlled Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Set Point SP] --> Sum1(("+"))
    PVFB[Process Variable PV] --> Sum1(("+"))
    Sum1 --> Err[Error e t]
    Err --> PBlock[Proportional Kc]
    Err --> IBlock[Integral Kc by tauI s]
    Err --> DBlock[Derivative Kc tauD s]
    PBlock --> Sum2(("+"))
    IBlock --> Sum2(("+"))
    DBlock --> Sum2(("+"))
    Sum2 --> Out[Controller Output u t]
    Out --> Plant[Process Plant Gp s]
    Plant --> Dist[Disturbance d]
    Dist --> PVOut[Process Variable PV]
    PVOut --> PVFB
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the PID Trainer Kit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SG[Set Point Generator]
        SG1[Step Input]
        SG2[Ramp Input]
        SG3[Square Pulse]
    end

    subgraph CT[Controller Module]
        C1[P Action Knob]
        C2[I Action Knob]
        C3[D Action Knob]
        C4[Auto Manual Switch]
    end

    subgraph PL[Process Simulation Module]
        P1[FOPDT Plant Analogue]
        P2[Integrating Plant]
        P3[Second Order Plant]
    end

    subgraph IND[Indicator Module]
        I1[Analog Meter PV]
        I2[Analog Meter OP]
        I3[CRO Output]
        I4[PC Data Acquisition]
    end

    SG --> CT
    CT --> PL
    PL --> IND
    IND --> CT
```

## 4.3 Step-Response Waveform Annotation (Schematic Sketch)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start 0 percent] --> B[10 percent at t sub r1]
    B --> C[90 percent at t sub r2]
    C --> D[Peak M sub p at t sub p]
    D --> E[2 percent Settling Band]
    E --> F[Final Steady State 100 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Annotation Guide for the CRO/Plot:**
> - $t_d$ — **Dead Time** (initial flat region)
> - $t_r$ — **Rise Time** ($10\% \rightarrow 90\%$)
> - $M_p$ — **Peak Overshoot** $(\text{Peak} - \text{Final})/\text{Final} \times 100\%$
> - $t_s$ — **Settling Time** (entry into $\pm 2\%$ band)
> - $e_{ss}$ — **Steady-State Error** (final value vs set-point)

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define a PID controller. List any two advantages of derivative action in a feedback loop.

**Model Answer:**
A PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller is a three-mode feedback controller whose output is a linear combination of the error, its time integral, and its time derivative, expressed as
$$u(t) = K_c\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}\right]$$
**Advantages of derivative action:**
1. It provides **anticipatory control** by acting on the rate of change of error, thereby reducing overshoot.
2. It **increases damping** in the closed-loop response, improving stability margins.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
What is "reset wind-up" in a PID controller? How is it prevented?

**Model Answer:**
Reset wind-up (integral wind-up) occurs when the controller output saturates at its upper or lower limit (typically 0–100%), yet the integral term continues to accumulate error. On saturation reversal, the controller takes a long time to unwind the integral, causing large overshoots.
**Prevention methods:**
1. **Anti-reset wind-up (clamping):** Stop integration when output saturates.
2. **Back-calculation:** Feed back the difference between actual and desired output and subtract it from the integral term.
3. **External reset feedback** from a valve positioner.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A process, when subjected to an open-loop step test, gave the following FOPDT data:
- Process gain $K_p = 2.0$
- Dead time $t_0 = 0.8\,\text{min}$
- Time constant $\tau = 4.0\,\text{min}$

Compute the PID settings using:
(i) Ziegler–Nichols open-loop (reaction curve) method, and
(ii) Cohen–Coon method.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Ziegler–Nichols Open Loop:**
$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \frac{\tau}{K_p t_0} = \frac{4.0}{(2.0)(0.8)} = \frac{4.0}{1.6} = 2.5 \\
\tau_I &= t_0 = 0.8\,\text{min} \\
\tau_D &= \frac{t_0}{2} = \frac{0.8}{2} = 0.4\,\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Cohen–Coon:**
$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \frac{\tau}{K_p t_0}\left[\frac{4}{3} + \frac{t_0}{4\tau}\right] = \frac{4.0}{1.6}\left[\frac{4}{3} + \frac{0.8}{16}\right] \\
&= 2.5 \times \left[1.3333 + 0.05\right] = 2.5 \times 1.3833 = 3.458 \\
\tau_I &= t_0\,\frac{32 + 6t_0/\tau}{13 + 8t_0/\tau} = 0.8\,\frac{32 + 6(0.2)}{13 + 8(0.2)} \\
&= 0.8 \times \frac{33.2}{14.6} = 0.8 \times 2.274 = 1.819\,\text{min} \\
\tau_D &= t_0\,\frac{4}{11 + 2t_0/\tau} = 0.8 \times \frac{4}{11.4} = 0.8 \times 0.3509 = 0.281\,\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Tabulated Comparison:**

| Parameter | Z-N Open Loop | Cohen–Coon |
|---|---|---|
| $K_c$ | 2.5 | 3.46 |
| $\tau_I$ | 0.80 min | 1.82 min |
| $\tau_D$ | 0.40 min | 0.28 min |

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, describe the experimental procedure to obtain a process reaction curve from the PID trainer. List the data to be recorded.

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus:** PID controller trainer kit, patch cords, CRO/PC-DAQ, stopwatch.

**Procedure:**
1. Connect the controller output to the process input, and the process output (PV) back to the controller input.
2. Switch the controller to **MANUAL** mode. Set the output to a steady value (e.g., 30%).
3. Allow the PV to stabilize. Note the steady PV value $PV_0$ and the controller output $u_0$.
4. Apply a **small step change** $\Delta u$ (e.g., 10% increase) in manual output. Start the stopwatch simultaneously.
5. Record PV readings at fixed intervals (e.g., every 5 or 10 s) until a new steady state $PV_\infty$ is reached.
6. Plot $y(t)$ vs $t$. From the S-shaped curve, draw the tangent at the inflection point.
7. The tangent's intersection with $PV_0$ gives **dead time $t_0$**. Its intersection with $PV_\infty$ gives the apparent time $t_1$. The process time constant is $\tau = t_1 - t_0$.
8. Calculate $K_p = (PV_\infty - PV_0)/\Delta u$.
9. Now switch to **AUTO** mode, set the Z-N tuned PID values, perturb the set point, and observe servo response. Record overshoot, rise time, settling time.

**Data to be recorded:** $u_0, PV_0, \Delta u, t$ vs $PV$ readings, $t_0, \tau, K_p$, and closed-loop response curves for servo and regulatory tests.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Using the Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop (ultimate cycle) method, derive the tuning relations for a PID controller. An experiment gives $K_u = 6.0$ and $P_u = 3.0\,\text{min}$. Calculate the PID settings.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Z-N Closed-Loop Relations:**
At the **ultimate gain $K_u$**, the closed-loop system oscillates with constant amplitude. The characteristic equation reduces to a marginally stable second-order form, and the period of sustained oscillation $P_u$ is twice the imaginary part of the pole. Empirically, Ziegler and Nichols observed that **one-quarter decay ratio** responses are obtained when the controller gain is set to about 60% of the ultimate gain, with the integral and derivative times scaled to fractions of the ultimate period. This yielded:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= 0.60 \, K_u \\
\tau_I &= 0.50 \, P_u \\
\tau_D &= 0.125 \, P_u
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Evaluation** (with $K_u = 6.0$ and $P_u = 3.0\,\text{min}$):
$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= 0.60 \times 6.0 = 3.6 \\
\tau_I &= 0.50 \times 3.0 = 1.5\,\text{min} \\
\tau_D &= 0.125 \times 3.0 = 0.375\,\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the effect of each PID mode on the closed-loop response of a process. Support with characteristic curves.

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Effect on Rise Time | Effect on Overshoot | Effect on $e_{ss}$ | Effect on Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P only** ($K_c\uparrow$) | Decreases | Increases | Reduces but never eliminates | Marginally decreases |
| **I only** ($\tau_I\downarrow$) | Decreases | Increases | Eliminates completely | Decreases |
| **D only** ($\tau_D\uparrow$) | Minor decrease | Strongly decreases | No effect | Increases (adds damping) |

**Characteristic Curves (qualitative description):**
1. **P-only controller:** Exponential rise with finite offset; increasing $K_c$ reduces offset but increases oscillation. At critical $K_c$, sustained oscillations occur; beyond, instability.
2. **PI controller:** Offset completely removed; slight overshoot; response is slower than pure P.
3. **PD controller:** Reduced overshoot, faster settling, but offset remains.
4. **PID controller:** Combines best of all — fast rise, minimal overshoot, zero offset, stable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** Many students write $e = PV - SP$ instead of $e = SP - PV$. This reverses the sign of $K_c$ in direct-acting vs reverse-acting loops — **cost: 1 mark minimum**.
> 2. **Unit consistency:** Mixing minutes and seconds for $\tau_I$ and $\tau_D$ without stating the unit — examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting derivative action sign:** Derivative acts on the rate of change of *error*, not of PV alone. If you write $d(PV)/dt$, you will lose **1 full mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the reaction-curve graphical construction:** Just stating the formulas without the tangent-line construction for $t_0$ and $\tau$ loses **2 marks** in the procedure question.
> 5. **Confusing servo vs regulatory response:** Servo = set-point change; Regulatory = disturbance change. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **PID equation (parallel form):** $u(t) = K_c e + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt + K_c \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt}$
- [ ] **Error sign:** $e = SP - PV$ (universally used in process control).
- [ ] **P-action** → proportional to **present** error → reduces rise time, leaves offset.
- [ ] **I-action** → proportional to **past** accumulated error → eliminates offset.
- [ ] **D-action** → proportional to **rate of change** of error → adds damping, anticipates.
- [ ] **FOPDT model:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-t_0 s}}{\tau s + 1}$ — the universal process model.
- [ ] **Z-N Open-Loop:** $K_c = \tau/(K_p t_0)$, $\tau_I = t_0$, $\tau_D = t_0/2$.
- [ ] **Z-N Closed-Loop (Ultimate Cycle):** $K_c = 0.6 K_u$, $\tau_I = 0.5 P_u$, $\tau_D = 0.125 P_u$.
- [ ] **Cohen–Coon:** Gives higher $K_c$ than Z-N open loop → faster disturbance rejection.
- [ ] **Reset wind-up** must be prevented using **anti-reset wind-up** or **back-calculation**.
- [ ] **Servo response** = set-point change test; **Regulatory response** = disturbance rejection test.
- [ ] **Performance metrics to record:** Rise time $t_r$, Settling time $t_s$, Overshoot $M_p$, Steady-state error $e_{ss}$.
- [ ] **Tuning rule of thumb:** If the loop oscillates → reduce $K_c$ or increase $\tau_I$ or reduce $\tau_D$.
- [ ] **Always** record FOPDT parameters ($K_p, t_0, \tau$) **before** switching to AUTO mode.
- [ ] **Lab Safety:** Never exceed 100% controller output; use **bumpless transfer** when switching A/M.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of the Tuning Parameters in the Response of a Process Control System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBL607 - REACTION ENGINEERING AND PROCESS CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of the Tuning Parameters in the Response of a Process Control System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of a **Process Control System** (PCS), **tuning parameters** refer to the adjustable gain coefficients of a **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** — namely the **Proportional Gain (Kc or Kp)**, the **Integral Gain (Ki)**, and the **Derivative Gain (Kd)** — whose numerical values directly govern the **transient response**, **steady-state behavior**, and **stability** of a closed-loop control system when subjected to a disturbance or set-point change.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme lab manual for **PCBBL607**, Module Experiment 2 mandates the empirical observation of how the manipulation of each of these three parameters individually modifies the characteristic response curves (e.g., step response) of a first-order plus dead time (FOPDT) process. The objective is to develop an intuitive engineering feel for **controller tuning** — the art of selecting Kp, Ki, and Kd such that the controlled variable (e.g., temperature, flow, level, or composition) rapidly and stably tracks the desired set-point with minimal oscillation and zero steady-state offset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCBBL607 – Module 2):**
> Students are required to perform real-time experiments on a pilot-scale process control trainer (e.g., a level/flow/temperature control rig) equipped with a PID controller. The student must deliberately vary Kc, τI (integral time), and τD (derivative time) and record the corresponding responses. Plots of process variable (PV) vs. time must be analyzed for **rise time (tr)**, **peak overshoot (Mp)**, **settling time (ts)**, **steady-state error (ess)**, and **oscillation period (Pc)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a hilly road and trying to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** (this is your **set-point**, SP). The car is the **process**, your foot on the accelerator is the **manipulated variable (MV)**, and the speedometer reading is the **process variable (PV)**.

- **Proportional Action (Kp):** This is like pressing the accelerator **harder in direct proportion to how far your speed deviates from 60 km/h**. If you are at 40 km/h, you press much harder than if you are at 55 km/h. But notice — when you reach exactly 60 km/h, you stop pressing entirely. The problem? On an uphill slope, the car might settle at 58 km/h because some throttle is needed just to maintain speed. This residual gap is the **steady-state offset (or bias)**.

- **Integral Action (Ki):** This is the **memory of past errors**. Your cruise control keeps a running tab of how long you have been below 60 km/h. Even a tiny persistent error accumulates and gradually forces you to press the accelerator a little more, eliminating the offset over time. Too much integral action, however, causes you to **over-correct** — slamming the accelerator even after the speed has already crossed 60, leading to oscillations and "hunting."

- **Derivative Action (Kd):** This is **anticipation based on the rate of change**. If you see the speed rapidly climbing toward 70 km/h, you ease off the accelerator *before* you actually overshoot. It is a **damping** or **braking** action that resists sudden changes and smooths the response. If Kd is too high, the system becomes sluggish and overly sensitive to noise (like reacting to every bump in the road).

> [!NOTE]
> **The PID control law** in the **ideal parallel (non-interacting) form** is mathematically expressed as:
>
> $$MV(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(t)\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$
>
> where **e(t) = SP − PV** is the error signal, **Kc** is the proportional gain (dimensionless or %/%), **τI** is the integral time (minutes or seconds per repeat), and **τD** is the derivative time (minutes or seconds).

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics for Response Evaluation

When tuning a controller, the KTU lab evaluator expects the student to identify and compute the following **time-domain specifications** from the recorded step response:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Response Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Vocabulary):**
> - **Rise Time (tr):** Time required for the PV to move from **10% to 90%** of its final value. Indicator of *speed*.
> - **Peak Overshoot (Mp):** Maximum excursion of PV beyond the final steady value, expressed as a **percentage**.
> - **Settling Time (ts):** Time required for the PV to remain within a **±2% (or ±5%)** band of the final value. Indicator of *speed of stabilization*.
> - **Steady-State Error (ess):** The final, persistent deviation between SP and PV after transients die out.
> - **Decay Ratio (DR):** Ratio of the magnitudes of successive oscillations (e.g., 1/4 for a well-tuned quarter-amplitude response).
> - **Period of Oscillation (Pc):** Time between two successive peaks.

**Units and Standard Symbols (to be used in your KTU lab record):**
- **Kc** → dimensionless (or %/%) — **Proportional Band (PB) = 100/Kc** (in %)
- **τI** → minutes/repeat (also called *reset time*) — **Ki = Kc/τI**
- **τD** → minutes — **Kd = Kc × τD**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Tuning Parameters — Engineering Roles

#### 2.1.1 Proportional Action (Kc or Kp)

The proportional term produces a controller output that is **directly proportional to the instantaneous error**. Mathematically:

$$MV_P(t) = K_c \cdot e(t)$$

**Mechanism of Action:**
- A larger Kc means the controller reacts more aggressively to any deviation.
- It **reduces the rise time** (faster response).
- It **decreases (but does not eliminate) the steady-state error**.
- It **increases the overshoot** and can drive the system to **instability** (sustained oscillations) if set too high.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often claim "increasing Kc eliminates offset." This is **incorrect**. Proportional control *alone* always leaves a residual offset (called *proportional bias*). Only the integral action can remove the steady-state error completely.

#### 2.1.2 Integral Action (τI or Ki)

The integral term produces an output that is proportional to the **time-integral of the past error**:

$$MV_I(t) = \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Mechanism of Action:**
- The integral term acts as a **memory** — it keeps accumulating as long as any error persists.
- Its primary benefit is the **elimination of steady-state error** (it pushes ess to zero for step inputs).
- It **slows down the response** (increases rise time and settling time).
- It **introduces phase lag**, which destabilizes the loop and **increases overshoot/oscillation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reciprocal Relationship:** A *smaller* τI (called *reset time*) means a *stronger* integral action. Some industrial controllers use **Ki (resets per minute) = 1/τI**; hence a low τI corresponds to a high Ki.

#### 2.1.3 Derivative Action (τD or Kd)

The derivative term acts on the **rate of change of the error**:

$$MV_D(t) = K_c \cdot \tau_D \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Mechanism of Action:**
- The derivative term is purely **anticipatory** — it cannot respond to a constant error (derivative of a constant is zero).
- It **adds phase lead**, counteracting the phase lag introduced by the integral term.
- It **reduces overshoot** and **improves damping** of oscillations.
- It is highly sensitive to **measurement noise** (since differentiation amplifies high-frequency noise).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Name** | **Formula / Relation** | **Effect when INCREASED** | **Effect when DECREASED** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K_c$ | Proportional Gain | $K_c = \frac{100}{PB}$ (PB = Proportional Band) | ↓ rise time, ↑ overshoot, ↓ ess (not zero), may destabilize | ↑ rise time, ↓ overshoot, ↑ ess, sluggish |
| $\tau_I$ | Integral Time (Reset Time) | $\tau_I = \frac{K_c}{K_i}$ | ↓ overshoot, ↓ ess, faster ess→0, ↑ oscillation | ↑ ess, ↑ stability, but slower ess removal |
| $\tau_D$ | Derivative Time | $\tau_D = \frac{K_d}{K_c}$ | ↓ overshoot, ↑ damping, better stability, noise-sensitive | More oscillatory, less damping, but faster |
| $e_{ss}$ | Steady-State Error (P-only) | $e_{ss} = \frac{R}{1+K_c K_p}$ | — | — |
| $M_p$ | Percent Overshoot | $M_p = \frac{y_{peak} - y_{\infty}}{y_{\infty}} \times 100$ | — | — |
| $t_r$ | Rise Time (0%→100%) | Read from graph | ↓ | ↑ |
| $t_s$ | Settling Time (±2%) | Read from graph | ↓ | ↑ |
| $\zeta$ | Damping Ratio (2nd-order approx.) | $\zeta \approx \frac{-\ln(M_p/100)}{\sqrt{\pi^2 + \ln^2(M_p/100)}}$ | Less oscillatory | More oscillatory |
| $P_c$ | Ultimate Period | Read from sustained-oscillation test | — | — |
| $K_{cu}$ | Ultimate Gain | $K_{cu} = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_u) \vert}$ at $\omega_u$ | — | — |

### 2.3 Ziegler-Nichols Tuning Rules (Continuous Cycling Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ziegler-Nichols (Z-N) Ultimate-Cycle Method** is the **most frequently tested tuning method** in KTU exams. Memorize the table below.

**Procedure:**
1. Set Ki = ∞ and Kd = 0 (i.e., disable integral and derivative).
2. Gradually increase Kc until the system exhibits **sustained, constant-amplitude oscillations** (a marginally stable limit cycle).
3. Record the **Ultimate Gain (Kcu)** at which this occurs and the corresponding **Period of Oscillation (Pc)**.

**Z-N Recommended Settings:**

| **Controller Type** | **Kc** | **τI** | **τD** |
|---|---|---|---|
| P-only | $0.5 K_{cu}$ | — | — |
| PI | $0.45 K_{cu}$ | $P_c / 1.2$ | — |
| PID | $0.60 K_{cu}$ | $P_c / 2$ | $P_c / 8$ |

### 2.4 Cohen-Coon Tuning Rules (Open-Loop Reaction-Curve Method)

For a process modelled as a **First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT)** system:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \cdot e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

where $K_p$ is the process gain, $\tau$ is the time constant, and $\theta$ is the dead time.

| **Controller** | **Kc** | **τI** | **τD** |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | $\frac{1}{K_p}\frac{\tau}{\theta}\left(1 + \frac{\theta}{3\tau}\right)$ | — | — |
| PI | $\frac{1}{K_p}\frac{\tau}{\theta}\left(0.9 + \frac{\theta}{12\tau}\right)$ | $\theta \frac{30 + 3\theta/\tau}{9 + 20\theta/\tau}$ | — |
| PID | $\frac{1}{K_p}\frac{\tau}{\theta}\left(1.33 + \frac{\theta}{4\tau}\right)$ | $\theta \frac{32 + 6\theta/\tau}{13 + 8\theta/\tau}$ | $\theta \frac{4}{11 + 2\theta/\tau}$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles taught in this experiment are foundational to **every industrial automation discipline**:

- **Chemical Process Industries:** Temperature control of CSTRs, level control in distillation columns, pH control in wastewater treatment.
- **Petrochemical Refineries:** Flow control in pipelines, pressure control in distillation overheads.
- **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing:** Batch reactor temperature profiling under FDA-validated PID loops.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler drum-level and steam-temperature control.
- **Robotics & Mechatronics:** Servo-motor position and velocity control.
- **Aerospace:** Flight-control surface actuation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Error for Proportional Control of a First-Order Plant

**Consider a unity-feedback closed loop with:**
- Plant: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$
- Controller: $G_c(s) = K_c$ (Proportional only)
- Set-point: $R(s) = \dfrac{A}{s}$ (a step of magnitude A)

**Step 1 — Write the closed-loop transfer function from R(s) to Y(s):**

$$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_c(s)G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)} = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau s + 1 + K_c K_p}$$

**Step 2 — Express Y(s) in terms of the input R(s):**

$$Y(s) = T(s) \cdot R(s) = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau s + 1 + K_c K_p} \cdot \frac{A}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Final Value Theorem to find the steady-state value of Y:**

$$y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot Y(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K_c K_p \, A}{s(\tau s + 1 + K_c K_p)}$$

$$y_{ss} = \frac{K_c K_p \, A}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the steady-state error:**

$$e_{ss} = A - y_{ss} = A - \frac{K_c K_p \, A}{1 + K_c K_p} = A\left[1 - \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}\right] = \frac{A}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = \frac{A}{1 + K_c K_p}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- As $K_c \to \infty$, $e_{ss} \to 0$ (theoretically). In practice, $K_c$ cannot be increased indefinitely because the loop becomes unstable.
- The factor $(1 + K_c K_p)$ is the **loop gain** at steady state.
- The **residual offset** proves that **P-control alone cannot eliminate steady-state error** for step inputs.

### 3.2 Derivation: Damping Ratio from Percent Overshoot

Given a second-order underdamped response, the percent overshoot is:

$$M_p = e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 1 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides (after dividing by 100):**

$$\ln\left(\frac{M_p}{100}\right) = -\frac{\zeta \pi}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Square both sides to eliminate the square-root:**

$$\ln^2\left(\frac{M_p}{100}\right) = \frac{\zeta^2 \pi^2}{1 - \zeta^2}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $\zeta^2$:**

$$\ln^2\left(\frac{M_p}{100}\right) (1 - \zeta^2) = \zeta^2 \pi^2$$

$$\ln^2\left(\frac{M_p}{100}\right) - \zeta^2 \ln^2\left(\frac{M_p}{100}\right) = \zeta^2 \pi^2$$

$$\ln^2\left(\frac{M_p}{100}\right) = \zeta^2 \left[\pi^2 + \ln^2\left(\frac{M_p}{100}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for ζ:**

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{-\ln(M_p/100)}{\sqrt{\pi^2 + \ln^2(M_p/100)}}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Suppose the recorded peak overshoot is **Mp = 25%**. Compute ζ.

**Step 1 —** Compute $\ln(0.25) = -1.3863$.

**Step 2 —** Compute $\pi^2 = 9.8696$ and $\ln^2(0.25) = 1.9218$.

**Step 3 —** Sum: $9.8696 + 1.9218 = 11.7914$.

**Step 4 —** Take square root: $\sqrt{11.7914} = 3.4340$.

**Step 5 —** Final result: $\zeta = \dfrac{1.3863}{3.4340} = 0.4037$.

This corresponds to an **underdamped** response with **moderate oscillation** — typical of a properly tuned industrial loop.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameter Variation on Response Characteristics

| **Adjustment** | **Rise Time (tr)** | **Overshoot (Mp)** | **Settling Time (ts)** | **Steady-State Error (ess)** | **Stability Margin** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Kc | Decrease | Increase | Slight change | Decrease (never zero) | Decrease |
| ↓ Kc | Increase | Decrease | Increase | Increase | Increase |
| ↑ τI (weaker integral) | Slight decrease | Decrease | Slight decrease | Increase | Increase |
| ↓ τI (stronger integral) | Increase | Increase | Increase | Decrease to zero | Decrease |
| ↑ τD (more derivative) | Slight decrease | Decrease | Decrease | No effect on ess | Increase (up to a point) |
| ↓ τD (less derivative) | Slight increase | Increase | Increase | No effect | Decrease |
| **P-only** | Fast | Moderate | Moderate | **Non-zero offset** | Stable if Kc small |
| **PI** | Slower than P | More than P | More than P | **Zero offset** | Less stable than P |
| **PID** | Fastest of the three | Lowest | Lowest | **Zero offset** | Best stability (if tuned well) |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Z-N PID Tuning from Ultimate-Cycle Data

**Problem Statement (modelled on a KTU lab scenario):**
During a continuous-cycling test on a temperature control loop, the ultimate gain was found to be $K_{cu} = 8.0$ %/% and the ultimate period was $P_c = 240$ seconds. Compute the Z-N recommended PID settings.

**Step 1 — Apply the Z-N formula for Kc (PID controller):**

$$K_c = 0.60 \times K_{cu} = 0.60 \times 8.0 = 4.8\ \%/\%$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Z-N formula for τI:**

$$\tau_I = \frac{P_c}{2} = \frac{240}{2} = 120\ \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Z-N formula for τD:**

$$\tau_D = \frac{P_c}{8} = \frac{240}{8} = 30\ \text{seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Express the final controller transfer function:**

$$G_c(s) = K_c\left[1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right] = 4.8\left[1 + \frac{1}{120 s} + 30 s\right]$$

**Step 5 — Verification by dimensional check:**
- $K_c$ is dimensionless (or %/%).
- $1/\tau_I$ has units of 1/s → integrates error to a dimensionless MV contribution. ✓
- $\tau_D \cdot s$ is dimensionless → differentiates the error to a dimensionless MV contribution. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Z-N tuning rule produces a **quarter-amplitude decay ratio** response, which is often considered *aggressive* for process control (high overshoot of ~25%). In practice, KTU lab evaluators often expect the student to **fine-tune** the Z-N values by reducing Kc by 10–20% for smoother industrial behavior.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Closed-Loop PID Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the standard **unity-feedback PID control loop** that you will encounter on the process control trainer in your PCBBL607 lab.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SumNode1{{Summation Junction e = SP - PV}}
    SumNode1 --> PID[PID Controller Gc of s]
    PID --> MV[Manipulated Variable MV]
    MV --> Process[Process Plant Gp of s]
    Process --> PV[Process Variable PV]
    PV --> SumNode1
    PV --> Out[Recorder or SCADA Display]
    Dist[Disturbance D] -.-> Process
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **set-point (SP)** is the desired value entered by the operator (e.g., 60 °C).
- The **summation junction** computes the error $e(t) = SP - PV$.
- The **PID controller** receives $e(t)$ and computes the controller output $MV(t)$ using the three-term formula.
- The **process** (heater, valve, motor, etc.) responds to $MV(t)$ and produces a new $PV(t)$.
- The **disturbance** $D$ (e.g., a sudden change in inlet temperature) is an unwanted input that the loop must reject.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a PID Controller (Internal Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PID_Controller
        E[Error e of t] --> Pterm[Proportional Path Kc times e of t]
        E --> Iterm[Integral Path Kc over tauI times integral e dt]
        E --> Dterm[Derivative Path Kc times tauD times de by dt]
        Pterm --> SumPID{{Sum of Three Terms}}
        Iterm --> SumPID
        Dterm --> SumPID
        SumPID --> MVout[Controller Output MV of t]
    end
```

### 4.3 Comparative Step-Response Curves (Effect of Tuning)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Underdamped High Kc] -->|Oscillates and overshoots| Result1[Long settling time, large Mp]
    B[Critically Damped Ideal Kc tauD] -->|Reaches SP quickly| Result2[Minimal overshoot, fast ts]
    C[Overdamped Low Kc] -->|Sluggish and slow| Result3[Long rise time, no overshoot but ess nonzero]
    D[With Integral Action] -->|Eliminates bias| Result4[ess reduced to zero]
    E[With Derivative Action] -->|Adds damping| Result5[Reduces Mp and oscillation]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture of a Typical Industrial Process Control Trainer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HardwareRig[Process Control Trainer Rig]
        Sensor[Sensor RTD or Thermocouple]
        Transmitter[Transmitter 4 to 20 mA]
        I2P[Current to Pressure Converter I to P]
        PCV[Pneumatic Control Valve]
        Tank[Process Tank Heated or Cooled]
    end
    subgraph Controller[Digital PID Controller]
        Input[Analog Input Channel]
        CPU[Microprocessor with PID Algorithm]
        Output[Analog Output Channel]
        HMI[Operator HMI Display]
    end
    Sensor --> Transmitter
    Transmitter --> Input
    Input --> CPU
    CPU --> Output
    Output --> I2P
    I2P --> PCV
    PCV --> Tank
    Tank --> Sensor
    CPU <--> HMI
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Examination Note:** When asked to "explain the experimental setup," you must describe (a) the **process** (e.g., a heated water tank with a heater coil), (b) the **measurement element** (RTD/thermocouple), (c) the **transmitter** (4–20 mA standard signal), (d) the **controller** (digital PID with HMI), and (e) the **final control element** (pneumatic control valve or SCR/relay for heater). The closed loop must be drawn and labeled with arrows for signal flow.

### 4.5 Decision Flowchart for Tuning Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Tuning Procedure] --> Q1{Is open-loop step test feasible?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| A[Perform Step Test]
    A --> B[Fit FOPDT Model Kp tau theta]
    B --> C[Apply Cohen-Coon or Lambda Tuning Rules]
    Q1 -->|No| D[Perform Closed-Loop Cycling Test]
    D --> E[Find Kcu and Pc]
    E --> F[Apply Ziegler-Nichols Rules]
    C --> G[Implement Settings on Controller]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Run Closed-Loop Test with Step Change in SP]
    H --> I{Response acceptable?}
    I -->|No, too oscillatory| J[Reduce Kc by 20 percent or increase tauD]
    I -->|No, too sluggish| K[Increase Kc by 20 percent or decrease tauI]
    I -->|Yes, accept| End[Record Final Kc tauI tauD and Response]
    J --> H
    K --> H
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the three tuning parameters of a PID controller and state the units of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The three tuning parameters of a Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller are:

1. **Proportional Gain (Kc):** A dimensionless quantity (or expressed in %/%) that multiplies the instantaneous error. It is the reciprocal of the **Proportional Band (PB = 100/Kc)**.
2. **Integral Time (τI):** Measured in **seconds or minutes per repeat** (sometimes called *reset time*). It is the time required for the integral action to repeat the contribution of the proportional action.
3. **Derivative Time (τD):** Measured in **seconds or minutes**. It determines how strongly the controller reacts to the rate of change of the error.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. What is meant by "steady-state error" in a process control loop? Why cannot proportional control alone eliminate it for a step input?**

**Model Answer:**

**Steady-state error (ess)** is the persistent difference between the set-point (SP) and the process variable (PV) after all transient disturbances have died out and the system has reached equilibrium.

For a unity-feedback system with proportional gain Kc and plant gain Kp, the steady-state error for a step input of magnitude A is derived from the Final Value Theorem as:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{A}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

This error is **non-zero for any finite Kc** because a finite controller output is required to maintain the process at the set-point, which in turn demands a non-zero error. To drive $e_{ss}$ to zero, the integral action is required, since it continuously accumulates the error and adjusts the controller output until the error vanishes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw a labelled block diagram of a closed-loop PID control system as implemented in the PCBBL607 process control trainer. Explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a step-test on a laboratory temperature control loop, the following FOPDT parameters were obtained: process gain $K_p = 2.5$ °C/%, time constant $\tau = 180$ s, and dead time $\theta = 30$ s. Using the **Cohen-Coon** tuning rules, calculate the PID settings ($K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$) suitable for this process. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks):**

The block diagram is given in Section 4.1. Each block is explained as follows:

- **Set-Point (SP):** The desired value of the controlled variable entered by the operator on the HMI of the digital PID controller. Example: 60 °C.
- **Summation Junction:** Computes the error $e(t) = SP - PV$.
- **PID Controller $G_c(s)$:** Implements the control law $MV(t) = K_c \left[e + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}\right]$.
- **Final Control Element (e.g., pneumatic valve or SCR):** Converts the electrical/pneumatic controller output into a physical manipulation of the process (e.g., opening/closing a steam valve).
- **Process Plant $G_p(s)$:** The physical system (e.g., heated tank) being controlled.
- **Sensor + Transmitter:** Measures the PV and converts it to a standard 4–20 mA signal.
- **Feedback Path:** Returns the measured PV to the summation junction, closing the loop.
- **Disturbance D(s):** Unwanted input (e.g., sudden ambient temperature change) that the loop must reject.

**(b) Cohen-Coon PID Calculations (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall the Cohen-Coon formulas for a PID controller:**

$$K_c = \frac{1}{K_p}\frac{\tau}{\theta}\left(1.33 + \frac{\theta}{4\tau}\right)$$

$$\tau_I = \theta\,\frac{32 + 6\theta/\tau}{13 + 8\theta/\tau}$$

$$\tau_D = \theta\,\frac{4}{11 + 2\theta/\tau}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio $\theta/\tau$:**

$$\frac{\theta}{\tau} = \frac{30}{180} = 0.1667$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Kc:**

$$K_c = \frac{1}{2.5}\cdot\frac{180}{30}\left(1.33 + \frac{0.1667}{4}\right)$$

$$K_c = \frac{1}{2.5}\cdot 6 \cdot \left(1.33 + 0.0417\right)$$

$$K_c = 2.4 \cdot 1.3717 = 3.292$$

**Step 4 — Calculate τI:**

$$\tau_I = 30 \cdot \frac{32 + 6(0.1667)}{13 + 8(0.1667)} = 30 \cdot \frac{32 + 1.000}{13 + 1.333}$$

$$\tau_I = 30 \cdot \frac{33.000}{14.333} = 30 \cdot 2.302 = 69.07\ \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate τD:**

$$\tau_D = 30 \cdot \frac{4}{11 + 2(0.1667)} = 30 \cdot \frac{4}{11.333} = 30 \cdot 0.3530 = 10.59\ \text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{K_c \approx 3.29\ \%/\%,\quad \tau_I \approx 69.1\ \text{s},\quad \tau_D \approx 10.6\ \text{s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **forget to compute the ratio θ/τ first** and substitute inconsistent units (e.g., minutes vs. seconds). Always use **one consistent time unit** (e.g., seconds) throughout.
> 2. The Cohen-Coon method is **only valid when 0.1 < θ/τ < 1.0**. Outside this range, use a different method (e.g., Z-N or lambda tuning).
> 3. Always **state the assumptions** of the FOPDT model in your answer to score full marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With the help of a clear sketch, explain the **Ziegler-Nichols ultimate-cycle (continuous cycling) method** of tuning a PID controller. List the recommended Z-N settings for P, PI, and PID controllers in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature control loop exhibits sustained oscillations with period $P_c = 6$ minutes when the controller gain is set to $K_{cu} = 5$. Design a **PID controller** using the Ziegler-Nichols rules. What qualitative changes would you observe in the response if the integral time were halved? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate-Cycle Method (7 marks):**

**Procedure:**
1. With the controller in **P-only mode** (set τI = ∞ and τD = 0), gradually increase the proportional gain Kc in small steps.
2. After each step, introduce a small set-point change and observe the response.
3. Continue increasing Kc until the system exhibits **sustained, constant-amplitude sinusoidal oscillations** (the system is *marginally stable*).
4. Record the gain at which this occurs as the **Ultimate Gain (Kcu)** and the period of the oscillation as the **Ultimate Period (Pc)**.
5. Using the Z-N formulas, calculate the desired controller parameters for P, PI, or PID action.

**Z-N Recommended Settings Table:**

| **Controller** | **Kc** | **τI** | **τD** |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | $0.5 K_{cu}$ | — | — |
| PI | $0.45 K_{cu}$ | $P_c/1.2$ | — |
| PID | $0.60 K_{cu}$ | $P_c/2$ | $P_c/8$ |

A sketch of the **sustained oscillation** should be shown — a clean sinusoid with constant peak-to-peak amplitude and well-defined period Pc.

**(b) PID Design from Z-N (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the Z-N PID formulas:**

$$K_c = 0.60 \times K_{cu} = 0.60 \times 5 = 3.0\ \%/\%$$

$$\tau_I = \frac{P_c}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3\ \text{minutes}$$

$$\tau_D = \frac{P_c}{8} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75\ \text{minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Final Z-N PID settings:**

$$\boxed{K_c = 3.0,\quad \tau_I = 3\ \text{min},\quad \tau_D = 0.75\ \text{min}}$$

**Step 3 — Effect of halving τI (from 3 min to 1.5 min):**

Since $\tau_I$ and the integral gain $K_i = K_c/\tau_I$ are **inversely related**, halving τI **doubles the integral action strength**. The qualitative changes in the response would be:

- **Faster elimination of steady-state error** (the offset vanishes in less time).
- **Increased overshoot** and **more pronounced oscillations** in the transient response.
- **Reduced stability margin** — the loop may become underdamped or even unstable if τI is reduced too aggressively.
- **Larger initial controller action** when a set-point change is applied.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. Many students write "$K_{cu}$" as "$K_{cu}$ with units" but do not specify the units. Always state the units explicitly (e.g., **%/%, minutes, °C**).
> 2. In part (b), the question is qualitative about *halving* τI. Many students mistakenly say it "increases τI" — **NO, halving means decreasing** τI, which **increases** the integral action strength. This is a **favourite trap** of KTU evaluators.
> 3. The Z-N method gives a **quarter-amplitude decay** response, which is considered *too oscillatory* for many chemical processes. Mention that **fine-tuning** (e.g., reducing Kc by 10–20%) is recommended in industrial practice.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Carry to Your Lab Exam):**

- **PID** = Proportional + Integral + Derivative. The output is a **weighted sum** of the error, its integral, and its derivative.
- **Proportional (Kc):** ↓ rise time, ↓ ess, ↑ overshoot, ↓ stability (if too high).
- **Integral (τI or Ki):** Eliminates steady-state error, but increases overshoot and oscillation.
- **Derivative (τD or Kd):** Adds damping, reduces overshoot, sensitive to noise, cannot act on a constant error.
- **P-only** always leaves an **offset** for step inputs.
- **PI** removes the offset but may oscillate.
- **PID** offers the best combination — but requires careful tuning.
- **Ziegler-Nichols (continuous cycling):** Find $K_{cu}$ and $P_c$ first, then plug into the table. **Kc = 0.6 Kcu, τI = Pc/2, τD = Pc/8** for PID.
- **Cohen-Coon (open-loop step test):** Need FOPDT parameters $K_p, \tau, \theta$. Valid for $0.1 < \theta/\tau < 1.0$.
- **Key Response Metrics:** $t_r$ (rise time), $M_p$ (overshoot), $t_s$ (settling time, ±2% band), $e_{ss}$ (steady-state error), $P_c$ (oscillation period).
- **Damping Ratio Formula:** $\zeta = \dfrac{-\ln(M_p/100)}{\sqrt{\pi^2 + \ln^2(M_p/100)}}$.
- **Steady-State Error (P-control):** $e_{ss} = A / (1 + K_c K_p)$.
- **Lab Report Must Include:** (i) Block diagram, (ii) Procedure, (iii) Tabulated readings, (iv) Response curves (PV vs. time), (v) Calculated $t_r, M_p, t_s, e_{ss}$ for each tuning setting, (vi) Comparative discussion of Kc, τI, τD effects.
- **Units Used in Lab:** Kc in %/%, τI and τD in seconds (or minutes), PV in °C / L/min / m³/h depending on the rig.
- **Tuning Sequence (Standard Practice):** Start with **P-only** → adjust Kc for desired response → add **I-action** (large τI, then decrease) → add **D-action** if needed for damping → fine-tune.
- **Trap to Avoid:** Reducing τI (strengthening integral) too much causes **integral windup** — the controller output saturates and the response becomes highly oscillatory. Use **anti-windup** mechanisms in real industrial controllers.
- **Typical Realistic Industrial Values:** $K_c$ between 0.5 and 10 (depends on loop); $\tau_I$ between 0.5 and 10 minutes for temperature loops; $\tau_D$ between 0 and 1 minute (often zero in practice to avoid noise amplification).

> **End of Notes — PCBBL607 Module 2 Experiment 2**
